questions.question: What is Android?
questions.title: What is Android?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android is an open-source operating system developed by Google, built on top of the Linux kernel. It is mainly designed for mobile devices, but is also used across a wide range of smart devices. Where Android Is Used :- Android powers multiple types of devices, including:

sections.heading: Where Android is used
items: Smartphones and tablets
Smart TVs (Android TV)
Smartwatches (Wear OS)
Cars (Android Auto)
IoT and embedded devices such as kiosks, POS systems, and smart displays

sections.heading: Programming Languages Supported :- Android supports application development using multiple programming languages:
items: Kotlin – Officially recommended by Google, modern, concise, and safe
Java – Original Android language with a large existing codebase
C++ – Used via the Android NDK for performance-critical tasks like games and media processing

questions.question: What is Context? How is it used?
questions.title: What is Context? How is it used?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Android, Context is an abstract class that provides access to application-specific resources and system services. It allowing components to interact with resources like layouts, strings, databases, preferences, and system-level services. Types of Context in Android :- Android provides three main types of Context, each used for different purposes.

sections.heading: Application Context
sections.content: - getApplicationContext() - Lives for the entire app lifecycle - Used for singletons, background work, and non-UI resources

sections.heading: Activity Context
sections.content: - Provided by an Activity - Tied to the Activity lifecycle - Used for UI work like dialogs and layout inflation

sections.heading: Service Context
sections.content: - Provided by a Service - Used for background operations - Not suitable for UI

questions.question: Tell all Android application components.
questions.title: Tell all Android application components.

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android application components are the core building blocks of an app. Each component has a specific role and lifecycle managed by the Android system. There are four main Android application components:

sections.heading: 1. Activity
items: Represents a single UI screen
Handles user interaction
Manages screen lifecycle

sections.heading: 2. Service
items: Performs background tasks
Runs without a user interface
Used for long-running operations

sections.heading: 3. Broadcast Receiver
items: Listens for system or app events
Responds to events like battery change or network status
Has no UI

sections.heading: 4. Content Provider
items: Manages and shares app data
Supports CRUD operations
Ensures secure data access between apps

questions.question: What is AndroidManifest.xml?
questions.title: What is AndroidManifest.xml?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: AndroidManifest.xml is a mandatory configuration file in every Android app that provides essential information to the Android system.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Declares app components (Activities, Services, Broadcast Receivers, Content Providers)
Defines required permissions (e.g., Internet, Camera)
Specifies app metadata (name, icon, theme, version)
Sets SDK requirements (minSdk, targetSdk)
Identifies the app's launch Activity

questions.question: What is Application class?
questions.title: What is Application class?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Application class in Android is a base class that represents the entire application. It is created before any Activity, Service, or other app component and remains alive as long as the application process exists. Why It Is Used:- The Application class is used to:

sections.heading: Why it is used
items: Maintain global application state
Initialize app-wide libraries (Firebase, analytics, DI, etc.)
Store objects that need to live throughout the app lifecycle
Access a global context

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Only one instance exists per app
Created once when the app starts
Destroyed when the app process is killed
Has the longest lifecycle in the app

sections.heading: How It Is Used:-
sections.content: Create a custom Application class and register it in the manifest.

questions.question: What is ADB in Android?
questions.title: What is ADB in Android?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ADB (Android Debug Bridge) is a command-line tool that allows developers to communicate with an Android device or emulator from a computer. It is mainly used for debugging, testing, and managing Android applications. Why It Is Used:- ADB helps developers to:

sections.heading: Why it is used
items: Install and uninstall apps
Run shell commands on a device
View logs and debug issues
Transfer files between device and system
Control devices during development

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Works with real devices and emulators
Supports app debugging and testing
Allows direct access to the device shell

sections.heading: Common ADB Commands
items: adb devices – List connected devices
adb install app.apk – Install an app
adb logcat – View device logs
adb shell – Access device shell

questions.question: What is AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool)?
questions.title: What is AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool)?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool) is a build tool used by Android to compile, package, and manage app resources such as layouts, images, and the AndroidManifest.xml file. Why It Is Used:- AAPT is used to:

sections.heading: Why it is used
items: Compile resource files (XML, images, strings)
Generate the R.java / R class for resource access
Package resources into the APK or AAB
Validate resource references and manifest entries

sections.heading: Key Responsibilities
items: Converts resources into a binary format
Links resources with application code
Ensures resource IDs are correctly assigned

questions.question: What is DEX file?
questions.title: What is DEX file?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A DEX (Dalvik Executable) file is a compiled file format used by Android to store an app's bytecode. It is generated from Java or Kotlin code and is executed by the Android Runtime (ART) on the device. Why It Is Used:- DEX files are used to:

sections.heading: Why it is used
items: Convert Java/Kotlin bytecode into a format optimized for Android
Reduce memory usage on mobile devices
Allow efficient execution on Android runtime

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Contains all compiled classes of an app
Optimized for low memory and performance
Generated during the build process from .class files

questions.question: What is Multidex?
questions.title: What is Multidex?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Multidex is an Android feature that allows an application to use more than one DEX file. It is required when an app exceeds the 65,536 method limit imposed on a single DEX file.

sections.heading: Why Multidex Is Needed
sections.content: Apps with many libraries (Firebase, Retrofit, Google Play Services, etc.) can cross the 65K method limit. Multidex splits code into multiple DEX files so the app can build and run correctly.

sections.heading: Example Configuration
sections.content: Enable Multidex

questions.question: What are processes in Android?
questions.title: What are processes in Android?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A process is a running instance of an app with its own memory space. Android runs each app in a separate Linux process to ensure security and stability.

sections.heading: Why Processes Are Used
items: Keeps apps isolated and secure
Manages memory and performance
Prevents one app crash from affecting others

sections.heading: Process Priority Types
items: Foreground – App in use (highest priority)
Visible – Partially visible app
Service – Background work (e.g., music)
Background – Not visible
Empty – No active components (lowest priority)

questions.question: Is it possible to run an Android app in multiple processes? How?
questions.title: Is it possible to run an Android app in multiple processes? How?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Yes, an Android application can run in multiple processes. By default, all components of an app run in a single process, but Android allows you to assign different components to different processes using the android:process attribute.

sections.heading: How to Run an App in Multiple Processes
sections.content: You can specify a custom process name in AndroidManifest.xml for a component.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: The :background prefix creates a private process for the app
That component runs in a separate process
The rest of the app continues to run in the main process

questions.question: What is StrictMode?
questions.title: What is StrictMode?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: StrictMode is a debugging tool that helps detect performance issues like disk/network calls on the main thread and memory leaks.

sections.heading: Purpose
items: Catch bad coding practices
Prevent ANRs
Improve app responsiveness

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Thread Policy: Monitors main-thread operations
VM Policy: Detects memory leaks

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Used only in development
Not recommended for production

questions.question: What is Lint?
questions.title: What is Lint?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Lint is a static code analysis tool in Android that helps developers identify and fix potential bugs, performance issues, and code quality problems in their apps before runtime.

sections.heading: Why It Is Used
items: Detect common coding mistakes
Identify performance and usability issues
Enforce best practices and standards
Prevent runtime crashes

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Runs at compile-time
Integrated in Android Studio
Helps maintain clean and efficient code

questions.question: What is Support Library? Why was it introduced?
questions.title: What is Support Library? Why was it introduced?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Android Support Library is a set of code libraries provided by Google to help developers use newer Android features on older versions of the platform. It provides backward-compatible versions of APIs, UI components, and utilities.

sections.heading: Why It Was Introduced
items: Fragmented Android versions: Not all devices run the latest Android OS.
Backward compatibility: Allows developers to use modern features on older devices.
Consistent UI and functionality: Ensures apps behave similarly across different Android versions.
Faster development: Provides ready-to-use components like RecyclerView, CardView, ViewPager, etc.

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Backward-compatible UI widgets (RecyclerView, CardView, Toolbar)
Support for newer APIs on older devices
Utility classes for tasks like notifications, media, and animations

questions.question: What is Doze Mode? What is App Standby?
questions.title: What is Doze Mode? What is App Standby?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Both Doze Mode and App Standby help save battery by limiting background work.

sections.heading: Doze Mode
items: Activates when device is idle, screen off
Delays background tasks, syncs, and network
Allows high-priority notifications

sections.heading: App Standby
items: Applies to unused apps
Restricts background jobs and network
Active apps are not affected

sections.heading: In Short
items: Doze Mode: Affects the whole device
App Standby: Affects unused apps only

questions.question: What is File, Class, and Activity in Android?
questions.title: What is File, Class, and Activity in Android?

sections.heading: File
items: Used to store data on the device (internal, external, cache)
Examples: text files, images, databases

sections.heading: Class
items: A blueprint written in Kotlin/Java
Defines variables and functions
All Android components are classes

sections.heading: Activity
items: Represents one screen with UI
Handles user interaction
Managed by the Android lifecycle

questions.question: How to change parameters in an app without app update?
questions.title: How to change parameters in an app without app update?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: You can update app behavior without releasing a new version using remote configuration.

sections.heading: Common Ways
items: Firebase Remote Config: Fetch key-value updates at runtime (UI text, feature flags)
Backend API: Load configurable values from your server
Dynamic Feature Modules: Enable or disable app features remotely

sections.heading: Benefits
items: No app update required
Easy feature toggles & A/B testing
Quick content or behavior changes

questions.question: What is Activity and its lifecycle?
questions.title: What is Activity and its lifecycle?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: An Activity is a single screen in Android that handles user interaction and displays the UI.

sections.heading: Activity Lifecycle
items: onCreate() – Initialize UI and resources when Activity is first created.
onStart() – Activity becomes visible.
onResume() – Activity gains focus; user can interact.
onPause() – Activity loses focus; pause tasks or save data.
onStop() – Activity no longer visible; release resources.
onDestroy() – Activity is destroyed; cleanup resources.
onRestart() – Activity restarts after being stopped.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Manages visibility, focus, and resources.
Prevents crashes and data loss.
Lifecycle methods guide proper handling of UI and background tasks.

questions.question: Difference between onCreate() and onStart()
questions.title: Difference between onCreate() and onStart()

sections.heading: onCreate()
items: Called once when the Activity is first created
Used for initial setup (UI initialization, variables, binding data)
Executes before the Activity becomes visible

sections.heading: onStart()
items: Called when the Activity becomes visible to the user
May be called multiple times (e.g., returning from background)
Prepares the Activity to enter the foreground

sections.heading: In Short
items: onCreate() → Initialization
onStart() → Visibility

questions.question: When is only onDestroy() called without onPause() and onStop()?
questions.title: When is only onDestroy() called without onPause() and onStop()?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Normally, onPause() and onStop() run before onDestroy(), but they can be skipped in rare cases:

sections.heading: Cases
items: Activity finishes early (e.g., finish() during onCreate())
System kills the process due to low memory
App crash or fatal exception

sections.heading: Key Point
items: onDestroy() is not always guaranteed
Do important cleanup in onPause() or onStop(), not onDestroy()

questions.question: Activity lifecycle when launched for the first time
questions.title: Activity lifecycle when launched for the first time

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: When an Android Activity is launched for the first time, it goes through the following lifecycle methods in order:

sections.heading: 1. onCreate()
sections.content: Called when the Activity is first created. Used to initialize UI, variables, and resources. Example: setContentView(R.layout.activity_main)

sections.heading: 2. onStart()
sections.content: Called when the Activity becomes visible to the user. Prepares the Activity to enter the foreground.

sections.heading: 3. onResume()
sections.content: Called when the Activity gains focus and the user can interact with it. Activity is now in the foreground and fully active.

questions.question: Activity lifecycle when back button is pressed
questions.title: Activity lifecycle when back button is pressed

sections.heading: Activity Lifecycle When Back Button Is Pressed
sections.content: When the user presses the Back button, the current Activity is finished and removed from the screen. The system calls the following lifecycle methods in order:

sections.heading: 1. onPause()
sections.content: - Called when the Activity loses focus - Pause ongoing tasks or save temporary data

sections.heading: 2. onStop()
sections.content: - Called when the Activity is no longer visible - Release resources not needed in background

sections.heading: 3. onDestroy()
sections.content: - Called before the Activity is completely destroyed - Final cleanup of resources

sections.heading: In Short
sections.content: Back Press → onPause() → onStop() → onDestroy()

questions.question: Activity lifecycle when launched again after back press
questions.title: Activity lifecycle when launched again after back press

sections.heading: Activity Lifecycle When Launched Again After Back Press
sections.content: If the user presses the Back button, the Activity is destroyed. When it is launched again, a new instance of the Activity is created. The lifecycle methods are called in this order:

sections.heading: 1. onCreate()
items: Activity is created again
UI and data are initialized

sections.heading: 2. onStart()
items: Activity becomes visible

sections.heading: 3. onResume()
items: Activity gains focus and becomes interactive

sections.heading: In Short
sections.content: After Back Press → Activity destroyed Launch Again → onCreate() → onStart() → onResume()

questions.question: Activity lifecycle when home button is pressed
questions.title: Activity lifecycle when home button is pressed

sections.heading: Activity Lifecycle When Home Button Is Pressed
sections.content: When the user presses the Home button, the Activity is not destroyed. It moves to the background. The system calls the following lifecycle methods:

sections.heading: 1. onPause()
items: Called when the Activity loses focus
Pause animations or save temporary data

sections.heading: 2. onStop()
items: Called when the Activity is no longer visible
Release resources not needed in background

sections.heading: Note
sections.content: The Activity remains in memory and can be resumed later.

sections.heading: In Short
sections.content: Home Press → onPause() → onStop() (Activity goes to background)

questions.question: Activity lifecycle when app returns from background
questions.title: Activity lifecycle when app returns from background

sections.heading: Activity Lifecycle When App Returns from Background
sections.content: When the app returns from the background (after pressing the Home button or switching apps), the existing Activity instance is brought back to the foreground. The system calls the following lifecycle methods:

sections.heading: 1. onRestart()
items: Called after the Activity was stopped
Prepares the Activity to start again

sections.heading: 2. onStart()
sections.content: - Activity becomes visible to the user

sections.heading: 3. onResume()
sections.content: - Activity gains focus and becomes interactive

sections.heading: In Short
sections.content: Return from Background → onRestart() → onStart() → onResume()

questions.question: Lifecycle when navigating from Activity A → Activity B
questions.title: Lifecycle when navigating from Activity A → Activity B

sections.heading: Activity A
items: onPause() → loses focus
onStop() → no longer visible

sections.heading: Activity B
items: onCreate() → created
onStart() → visible
onResume() → interactive

sections.heading: Flow
sections.content: A: onPause → onStop B: onCreate → onStart → onResume Note

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Activity A stays in memory
Pressing Back destroys B and resumes A

questions.question: Lifecycle when pressing back from Activity B → Activity A
questions.title: Lifecycle when pressing back from Activity B → Activity A

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: When the user presses the Back button on Activity B, it is finished, and the system returns to Activity A. The lifecycle methods of both Activities are called as follows: Activity B

sections.heading: Activity B
items: onPause() → loses focus
onStop() → not visible
onDestroy() → finished

sections.heading: Activity A
items: onRestart() → coming back
onStart() → visible
onResume() → interactive

sections.heading: Flow
sections.content: onPause → onStop → onDestroy A: onRestart → onStart → onResume

questions.question: How to preserve activity state during screen rotation?
questions.title: How to preserve activity state during screen rotation?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: When an Activity is rotated, it is destroyed and recreated. To preserve state:

sections.heading: Common Ways
items: onSaveInstanceState(): Save small UI data (text, selections)
ViewModel: Keeps UI data across rotations

sections.heading: Key Points
items: onSaveInstanceState() → temporary UI state
ViewModel → long-lived UI data
Avoid manual config handling unless necessary

questions.question: What is savedInstanceState Bundle?
questions.title: What is savedInstanceState Bundle?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: savedInstanceState is a Bundle used to save and restore UI state when an Activity is recreated (e.g., screen rotation).

sections.heading: How It's Used
items: Save data in onSaveInstanceState()
Restore it in onCreate() or onRestoreInstanceState()

sections.heading: Key Points
items: null on first launch
Stores small UI data only
Helps restore state after rotation or system kill

questions.question: Difference between Intent and Bundle
questions.title: Difference between Intent and Bundle

sections.heading: Intent
items: Used to start Activities/Services or send broadcasts
Can carry data and navigation info

sections.heading: Bundle
items: A key-value data container
Used to pass data via Intent or save state

sections.heading: In Short
items: Intent = navigation + data
Bundle = data only

questions.question: What are launchModes?
questions.title: What are launchModes?

sections.heading: What Are Launch Modes?
sections.content: Launch modes control how an Activity is created and managed in the back stack.

sections.heading: Types
items: standard – Creates a new instance every time (default)
singleTop – Reuses the Activity if it is already at the top
singleTask – Only one instance in a task; brings existing one to front
singleInstance – Only one instance; runs in a separate task

sections.heading: In Short
sections.content: Launch modes define how Activity instances behave in the back stack.

questions.question: Explain standard launchMode
questions.title: Explain standard launchMode

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: standard is the default launch mode for all Activities in Android.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Every time the Activity is started, a new instance is created and pushed onto the back stack.
Multiple instances of the same Activity can exist simultaneously in the back stack.
Does not reuse any existing instance.

sections.heading: Example
sections.code:
// Start MainActivity multiple times
val intent = Intent(this, MainActivity::class.java)
startActivity(intent)
startActivity(intent)

sections.heading: Note
items: Result: Two separate instances of MainActivity are now in the back stack.

questions.question: Explain singleTop launchMode
questions.title: Explain singleTop launchMode

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: singleTop is a launch mode that reuses the Activity if it is already at the top of the back stack, instead of creating a new instance.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: If the Activity is not at the top, a new instance is created (like standard mode).
If the Activity is at the top, no new instance is created; onNewIntent() is called with the new Intent.
Prevents multiple copies of the same Activity when repeatedly launching it from the top.

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is taskAffinity?
questions.title: What is taskAffinity?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: taskAffinity is an attribute of an Activity that defines which task the Activity prefers to belong to. It allows Activities from the same or different apps to be associated with a particular task.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: By default, all Activities of an app share the same taskAffinity (usually the app package name).
You can set a custom taskAffinity in AndroidManifest.xml to control task behavior.
When launching an Activity, the system tries to place it in a task with the same taskAffinity.

sections.heading: Note
items: SpecialActivity will prefer to run in a task named com.example.specialtask.
Useful for creating separate tasks for specific Activities (e.g., for multi-window apps or notifications).

questions.question: What is installLocation tag?
questions.title: What is installLocation tag?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The installLocation tag in AndroidManifest.xml defines where the app is installed: internal or external storage. Values:

sections.heading: Values
items: internalOnly → Install on internal storage (default).
preferExternal → Prefer SD card, fallback to internal.
auto → System chooses the best location.

sections.heading: Key Point
sections.content: Controls storage use; some features may not work on SD card.

questions.question: Relationship between Activity and Fragment lifecycle
questions.title: Relationship between Activity and Fragment lifecycle

sections.heading: Introduction
items: Fragments exist inside an Activity, so their lifecycle is closely tied to the host Activity.
Fragment lifecycle events occur alongside the Activity, but Fragments have extra callbacks like onAttach(), onCreateView(), and onDetach() for managing UI.

sections.heading: Lifecycle Mapping (Simplified):
items: Activity onCreate() → Fragment onAttach() → onCreateView()
Activity onStart() → Fragment onStart()
Activity onResume() → Fragment onResume()
Activity onPause() → Fragment onPause()
Activity onStop() → Fragment onStop()
Activity onDestroy() → Fragment onDestroyView() → onDestroy() → onDetach()

sections.heading: Key Points:
items: Fragment depends on Activity but can manage its own UI.
Proper lifecycle handling prevents memory leaks and ensures smooth UI updates.

questions.question: When Activity A starts Activity B, explain the lifecycle order
questions.title: When Activity A starts Activity B, explain the lifecycle order

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: When Activity A starts Activity B, both Activities go through specific lifecycle callbacks as the system manages focus and visibility.

sections.heading: Activity A (Current Activity)
items: onPause() – Activity A loses focus because Activity B is coming to the foreground.
onStop() – Called if Activity B fully covers Activity A. Activity A is now in the background.

sections.heading: Activity B (New Activity)
items: onCreate() – Activity B is created and UI is initialized.
onStart() – Activity B becomes visible.
onResume() – Activity B gains focus and user can interact with it.

sections.heading: Lifecycle Flow (Simplified)
sections.code:
Activity A: onPause → onStop
Activity B: onCreate → onStart → onResume

questions.question: How to know configChange happens in onDestroy?
questions.title: How to know configChange happens in onDestroy?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: In Android, an Activity may be destroyed either due to user action or a configuration change (like screen rotation). To differentiate in onDestroy(), use isChangingConfigurations().

sections.heading: How It Works
items: isChangingConfigurations() returns true if the Activity is being destroyed due to a configuration change.
Returns false if destroyed for other reasons (e.g., user finishing the Activity or system reclaiming memory).

sections.heading: Example

questions.question: What is Fragment?
questions.title: What is Fragment?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Fragment is a modular part of an Activity with its own UI and lifecycle, allowing reusable and flexible screens.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Represents part of a screen
Can be added, removed, or replaced dynamically
Has its own lifecycle tied to the Activity
Supports independent UI and logic

questions.question: Fragment Lifecycle Methods
questions.title: Fragment Lifecycle Methods

sections.heading: Introduction
items: onAttach(Context) – Fragment attached to Activity; use for context-related init
onCreate(Bundle?) – Initialize Fragment (non-UI tasks)
onCreateView(LayoutInflater, ViewGroup?, Bundle?) – Inflate UI layout
onViewCreated(View, Bundle?) – After view is created; bind views & listeners
onStart() – Fragment becomes visible
onResume() – Fragment gains focus and is interactive
onPause() – Fragment loses focus; pause UI updates/animations
onStop() – Fragment no longer visible; release unnecessary resources
onDestroyView() – View hierarchy destroyed; clean up bindings
onDestroy() – Cleanup Fragment instance
onDetach() – Fragment detached from Activity; final cleanup

questions.question: Why is it recommended to use only the default constructor in Fragment?
questions.title: Why is it recommended to use only the default constructor in Fragment?

sections.heading: Introduction
items: System Recreation: Android recreates Fragments on rotation or low memory; only the default constructor is called.
Safe Data Passing: Use setArguments() with a Bundle to pass data, preserved across configuration changes.

sections.heading: Recommended Approach
Recommended Approach
sections.content: Key Points: Use default constructor + Bundle to ensure safe Fragment recreation.

questions.question: Fragment lifecycle when launched
questions.title: Fragment lifecycle when launched

sections.heading: Sequence:
items: onAttach() → attached to Activity
onCreate() → initialize non-UI data
onCreateView() → inflate UI
onViewCreated() → setup views/listeners
onStart() → visible
onResume() → active and interactive

sections.heading: Key Points:
items: Lifecycle tied to host Activity
Ensures UI readiness and efficient resource use

questions.question: Fragment lifecycle when back button is pressed
questions.title: Fragment lifecycle when back button is pressed

sections.heading: Sequence:
items: onPause() → loses focus
onStop() → not visible
onDestroyView() → UI destroyed
onDestroy() → instance destroyed
onDetach() → detached from Activity

sections.heading: Key Points:
items: Back press removes Fragment UI and instance
Use ViewModel or savedInstanceState to preserve data
Proper cleanup prevents memory leaks

questions.question: Explain Android Service lifecycle
questions.title: Explain Android Service lifecycle

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Service in Android has a lifecycle that is managed by the system. The lifecycle differs depending on how the Service is started.

sections.heading: Started Service (via startService())
items: onCreate() – Called once when the Service is created
onStartCommand() – Called every time startService() is called; returns how the service behaves if killed
onDestroy() – Called when the Service is stopped via stopSelf() or stopService()

sections.heading: Bound Service (via bindService())
items: onCreate() – Service is created
onBind() – Client binds to the Service; returns an IBinder
onUnbind() – All clients have unbound
onDestroy() – Service is destroyed

sections.heading: Key Points
items: onCreate() is called only once; onStartCommand() can be called multiple times
A started service runs independently; a bound service lives as long as a client is bound
Always call stopSelf() or stopService() for started services to avoid memory leaks

questions.question: What is Service?
questions.title: What is Service?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Service is an Android application component that performs long-running operations in the background without a user interface. It continues running even if the user switches to another app.

sections.heading: Types of Services
items: Started Service – Runs indefinitely until it stops itself (e.g., downloading a file)
Bound Service – Allows components to bind and interact with it via IBinder
Foreground Service – Runs with a visible notification (e.g., music playback)

sections.heading: Common Use Cases
items: Playing music in the background
Syncing data with a server
Handling network requests

questions.question: On which thread does a Service run in Android?
questions.title: On which thread does a Service run in Android?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: By default, a Service runs on the main (UI) thread of its hosting process. It does NOT create its own thread automatically.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Default Service runs on the main thread — blocking operations will cause ANR
You must manually create a background thread (e.g., using coroutines, Thread, or Executor) for heavy work
IntentService (deprecated) used to run on a worker thread by default
Use WorkManager or coroutines for background work in modern Android

questions.question: Service vs IntentService. When to prefer which?
questions.title: Service vs IntentService. When to prefer which?

sections.heading: Service
items: Runs on the main thread by default
Must manage threading manually
Suitable for long-running tasks needing manual control (e.g., music playback)
Stopped manually using stopSelf() or stopService()

sections.heading: IntentService (Deprecated)
items: Runs on a worker thread automatically
Handles one request at a time in a queue
Stops itself after all work is done
Deprecated in API 30 — use WorkManager instead

sections.heading: When to Prefer
items: Service: When you need continuous background work like streaming or ongoing connections
IntentService: Was preferred for one-off tasks; now replaced by WorkManager or coroutines

questions.question: What is a Foreground Service?
questions.title: What is a Foreground Service?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Foreground Service is a Service that performs an operation noticeable to the user. It must display a persistent notification and has higher priority than background services, making it less likely to be killed by the system.

sections.heading: Common Use Cases
items: Music or podcast playback
Location tracking (navigation apps)
File download/upload with progress

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Must call startForeground() with a Notification
Notification cannot be dismissed by the user while Service runs
Required for background location in Android 10+
Must declare FOREGROUND_SERVICE permission in manifest

questions.question: What is a JobScheduler?
questions.title: What is a JobScheduler?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: JobScheduler is an Android API (API 21+) that schedules background tasks to run under specific conditions, helping optimize battery and system performance.

sections.heading: When It Runs Jobs
items: When network is available (Wi-Fi or any)
When device is charging
When device is idle
At a specified time interval

sections.heading: Key Points
items: System batches jobs together for efficiency
More battery-efficient than manual background services
WorkManager is the modern recommended alternative
Jobs are not guaranteed to run at exact times

questions.question: How does WorkManager guarantee task execution?
questions.title: How does WorkManager guarantee task execution?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: WorkManager guarantees task execution by persisting work to a local database. Even if the app is killed or the device restarts, the work will be rescheduled and executed when conditions are met.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Work is stored in a Room database — survives app death and device reboot
WorkManager picks the best underlying implementation (JobScheduler, AlarmManager, etc.) based on API level
Constraints (network, charging) are respected before execution
Retries automatically on failure using backoff policies

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Suitable for deferrable, guaranteed background work
Not for real-time or exact-timing tasks
Supports chaining tasks and parallel execution

questions.question: WorkManager vs JobScheduler
questions.title: WorkManager vs JobScheduler

sections.heading: WorkManager
items: Recommended by Google for all background work
Works on API 14+ (uses JobScheduler internally on API 23+)
Persists work across reboots and app death
Supports chaining, parallel work, and input/output data
Simpler API with Kotlin coroutines support

sections.heading: JobScheduler
items: Available from API 21+
Does not persist across device reboots by default
Lower-level API; more boilerplate
No support for chaining or complex task graphs
Battery-efficient but less feature-rich

sections.heading: Prefer
items: WorkManager for almost all use cases — it wraps JobScheduler internally
JobScheduler only when targeting specific low-level scheduling behavior

questions.question: WorkManager types of requests, methods, minimum repeat interval
questions.title: WorkManager types of requests, methods, minimum repeat interval

sections.heading: Types of Work Requests
items: OneTimeWorkRequest – Executes the task once
PeriodicWorkRequest – Executes the task repeatedly at a set interval

sections.heading: Key Methods
items: WorkManager.enqueue() – Schedule a one-time task
WorkManager.enqueueUniquePeriodicWork() – Schedule periodic unique work
WorkManager.cancelWorkById() – Cancel a specific task
setConstraints() – Define conditions (network, charging, etc.)

sections.heading: Minimum Repeat Interval
items: PeriodicWorkRequest has a minimum interval of 15 minutes
This is an Android OS restriction to prevent battery drain
Cannot be configured to run more frequently than 15 minutes

questions.question: How to make a service persist even after application is killed?
questions.title: How to make a service persist even after application is killed?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: To keep a service running after the app is killed, you need to use specific strategies depending on your use case.

sections.heading: Approaches
items: Foreground Service: Use startForeground() — system is much less likely to kill it
Return START_STICKY from onStartCommand() — system restarts the service after killing it
WorkManager: For deferrable tasks, it reschedules automatically after app death
BroadcastReceiver with BOOT_COMPLETED: Restart the service after device reboot

sections.heading: Key Points
items: No service is 100% guaranteed to persist — system can kill any process under memory pressure
Foreground Service + START_STICKY is the most reliable combination
Avoid keeping services alive unnecessarily — it drains battery

questions.question: Why is there a need for JobIntentService?
questions.title: Why is there a need for JobIntentService?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: JobIntentService was introduced to handle background work that is compatible with both older Android versions and Android 8.0 (Oreo) background execution limits.

sections.heading: Why It Was Needed
items: Android 8.0 introduced strict background service limits — apps could no longer start background services freely
IntentService was not compatible with these new restrictions
JobIntentService uses JobScheduler on API 26+ and falls back to a regular service on older versions
Provides a unified API that works across API levels

sections.heading: Current Status
items: JobIntentService is now deprecated
WorkManager is the recommended replacement for all deferred background work

questions.question: Notification-related changes in Oreo
questions.title: Notification-related changes in Oreo

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android 8.0 (Oreo) introduced major notification changes to give users more control and improve organization.

sections.heading: Key Changes
items: Notification Channels (mandatory): All notifications must be assigned to a channel (e.g., Messages, Alerts). Without a channel, notifications are not shown on API 26+
Channel-level settings: Users can control sound, vibration, and importance per channel
Notification dots: Apps show a dot on the launcher icon when there are unread notifications
Snooze notifications: Users can snooze individual notifications
Background service restrictions: Apps cannot start background services; must use JobIntentService or WorkManager

sections.heading: Developer Action Required
items: Create NotificationChannel before posting any notification
Channel creation is ignored on older versions — safe to call on all API levels

questions.question: How to handle background service and execution limits with Oreo updates?
questions.title: How to handle background service and execution limits with Oreo updates?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android 8.0 (Oreo) imposed strict background execution limits to improve battery life. Apps can no longer freely run background services when the app is not in the foreground.

sections.heading: Key Restrictions
items: Background services are stopped a few minutes after the app goes to background
Implicit broadcast receivers are restricted (cannot register most in manifest)
startService() throws an IllegalStateException if called from background

sections.heading: How to Handle Them
items: Foreground Service: Use startForegroundService() + call startForeground() within 5 seconds
WorkManager: For deferrable background tasks — schedules with system constraints
JobScheduler / JobIntentService: For scheduled background work (now replaced by WorkManager)
FCM High Priority Push: Wake up app for time-sensitive tasks via push notification

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Use WorkManager for almost all background work — it handles API-level differences automatically
Only use Foreground Service when the task is user-noticeable and ongoing

questions.question: Difference between thread and coroutines
questions.title: Difference between thread and coroutines

sections.heading: Thread
items: OS-level construct — each thread has its own stack (~1MB memory)
Blocking: when waiting (e.g., I/O), the thread is blocked and wastes resources
Expensive to create and context-switch
Managed by the OS scheduler

sections.heading: Coroutines
items: Lightweight — thousands can run on a few threads
Suspendable: suspend instead of blocking; the thread is freed for other work
Managed by the Kotlin runtime, not the OS
Structured concurrency with scopes (CoroutineScope, viewModelScope, lifecycleScope)

sections.heading: In Short
items: Thread = heavy, blocking, OS-managed
Coroutine = lightweight, suspending, Kotlin-managed
Prefer coroutines for async work in modern Android

questions.question: Difference between concurrency vs parallelism
questions.title: Difference between concurrency vs parallelism

sections.heading: Concurrency
items: Multiple tasks make progress by interleaving on the same or fewer cores
Tasks are in progress at the same time but not necessarily running simultaneously
Example: switching between two tasks on a single CPU core

sections.heading: Parallelism
items: Multiple tasks run at the exact same instant on multiple CPU cores
Requires multi-core hardware
Example: two tasks executing simultaneously on two separate cores

sections.heading: In Short
items: Concurrency = dealing with multiple tasks at once (structure)
Parallelism = doing multiple tasks at once (execution)
Parallelism is a subset of concurrency

questions.question: What is a Thread, Handler, Looper, and Message Queue?
questions.title: What is a Thread, Handler, Looper, and Message Queue?

sections.heading: Thread
items: A unit of execution that runs code independently
Android apps start with one main thread (UI thread)

sections.heading: Looper
items: Keeps a thread alive by running a message loop
Attaches to a thread and processes messages from its queue one at a time
Main thread has a Looper by default; background threads do not

sections.heading: Message Queue
items: A queue of Messages and Runnables managed by the Looper
Processes items in FIFO order

sections.heading: Handler
items: Sends and processes Messages/Runnables to a thread's MessageQueue
Used to post work to the main thread from a background thread
Linked to the Looper of the thread it is created on

questions.question: Explain Looper, Handler, and HandlerThread
questions.title: Explain Looper, Handler, and HandlerThread

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Together these three enable communication between threads and allow background threads to process messages in a loop.

sections.heading: Looper
items: Runs an infinite message loop on a thread
Reads messages from the MessageQueue and dispatches them to the Handler
Looper.prepare() + Looper.loop() sets it up on any thread

sections.heading: Handler
items: Tied to a specific thread's Looper
post(Runnable) or sendMessage() schedules work on that thread
Commonly used to update UI from a background thread

sections.heading: HandlerThread
items: A Thread subclass that has a built-in Looper
Avoids manual Looper.prepare() boilerplate
Useful for serial background task processing (e.g., camera operations)

questions.question: Different methods of concurrency: ExecutorService vs CachedThreadPool vs FixedThreadPool vs AsyncTask vs HandlerThread
questions.title: Different methods of concurrency on Android

sections.heading: ExecutorService
items: Interface for managing a pool of threads
Base for all thread pool implementations
submit() returns a Future for result/cancellation

sections.heading: CachedThreadPool
items: Creates new threads as needed; reuses idle ones
No limit on thread count — risky for heavy workloads
Good for many short-lived tasks

sections.heading: FixedThreadPool
items: Fixed number of threads; tasks queue when all threads are busy
Predictable resource usage
Good for CPU-bound tasks with known concurrency

sections.heading: AsyncTask (Deprecated)
items: Simplified background + UI thread work
Deprecated in API 30 due to context leak and serial execution issues
Replace with coroutines or Executor

sections.heading: HandlerThread
items: Single background thread with a Looper
Good for serialized sequential background work
Used in Camera2 API, media operations

questions.question: Thread.start() vs run(), submit vs execute, Callable vs Runnable
questions.title: Thread.start() vs run(), submit vs execute, Callable vs Runnable

sections.heading: start() vs run()
items: start() – Creates a new thread and runs run() on it
run() – Executes on the current thread (no new thread is created)
Always use start() to run code concurrently

sections.heading: execute() vs submit()
items: execute(Runnable) – Fire and forget; no return value
submit(Callable/Runnable) – Returns a Future to get result or cancel
Use submit() when you need the result or to handle exceptions

sections.heading: Runnable vs Callable
items: Runnable – run() returns void; cannot throw checked exceptions
Callable – call() returns a value; can throw checked exceptions
Use Callable when a result is needed from the background task

questions.question: ThreadPool advantages
questions.title: ThreadPool advantages

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A ThreadPool manages a collection of reusable threads, avoiding the overhead of creating and destroying threads for each task.

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Thread reuse: Threads are reused instead of created/destroyed repeatedly — saves time and memory
Controlled concurrency: Limits the number of active threads to prevent resource exhaustion
Task queuing: Excess tasks are queued and executed when a thread becomes free
Better performance: Reduces CPU overhead of thread lifecycle management
Easier management: Provides submit(), shutdown(), and Future APIs for task control

questions.question: Daemon threads vs user threads
questions.title: Daemon threads vs user threads

sections.heading: User Thread
items: Normal threads created by default
JVM waits for all user threads to finish before exiting
Used for main application logic

sections.heading: Daemon Thread
items: Background/support threads (e.g., GC thread)
JVM exits without waiting for daemon threads to finish
Set via thread.setDaemon(true) before starting
Used for background housekeeping tasks

sections.heading: In Short
items: User thread = keeps JVM alive
Daemon thread = dies when all user threads finish
Do not use daemon threads for critical tasks

questions.question: How to run parallel tasks and get a callback when all are complete?
questions.title: How to run parallel tasks and get a callback when all are complete?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Several approaches exist to run tasks in parallel and be notified when all finish.

sections.heading: Using Kotlin Coroutines (Recommended)
items: Use async {} for each task inside a coroutineScope
Call awaitAll() to wait for all to complete
Runs in parallel on Dispatchers.IO or Default

sections.heading: Example
Other Approaches
items: CountDownLatch: latch.await() blocks until countDown() called N times
ExecutorService + invokeAll(): Submits all Callables and blocks until done
RxJava zip(): Combines multiple observables and emits when all complete

questions.question: How to download files in parallel?
questions.title: How to download files in parallel?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: To download multiple files simultaneously, use parallel background execution to avoid blocking the UI thread.

sections.heading: Using Coroutines (Recommended)
items: Launch separate async {} blocks for each file
Use Dispatchers.IO which is optimized for I/O operations
Collect results with awaitAll()

sections.heading: Using FixedThreadPool
items: Create a FixedThreadPool with desired thread count
Submit each download as a Runnable or Callable
Use invokeAll() to wait for completion

sections.heading: Key Points
items: For production: use WorkManager for download tasks — handles retries and survives app death
Libraries like OkHttp and Retrofit handle threading internally
Always show download progress via a Foreground Service for large files

questions.question: How does AsyncTask internally work? Cons of using AsyncTask?
questions.title: How does AsyncTask internally work? Cons of using AsyncTask?

sections.heading: How It Works Internally
items: Uses a SerialExecutor by default (tasks execute one at a time in a queue)
Background work runs on a shared thread pool
Results are posted back to the main thread via a Handler
Callbacks: onPreExecute() → doInBackground() → onPostExecute()

sections.heading: Cons
items: Memory leak: Holds an implicit reference to the outer Activity — if Activity is destroyed, leak occurs
Serial by default: Tasks don't run in parallel unless you use THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR explicitly
Lifecycle unaware: Continues running even if Activity is destroyed, causing crashes
No error handling: Exceptions in doInBackground() are silently swallowed
Deprecated in API 30: Google recommends coroutines or Executor instead

questions.question: Does AsyncTask.execute() make threads in parallel or serial?
questions.title: Does AsyncTask.execute() make threads in parallel or serial?

sections.heading: Answer
sections.content: By default, AsyncTask.execute() runs tasks serially (one at a time) on a single background thread using SerialExecutor.

sections.heading: Details
items: Before API 11: Tasks ran in parallel on a thread pool
API 11 and later: Changed to serial execution by default to avoid concurrency bugs
To run in parallel, use: asyncTask.executeOnExecutor(AsyncTask.THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR)

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Default = serial; parallel requires explicit THREAD_POOL_EXECUTOR
AsyncTask is deprecated — prefer coroutines for both serial and parallel work

questions.question: Cause of memory leak during AsyncTask
questions.title: Cause of memory leak during AsyncTask

sections.heading: Root Cause
sections.content: AsyncTask holds an implicit reference to its enclosing class (usually an Activity or Fragment). If the task is still running when the Activity is destroyed (e.g., on rotation), the Activity cannot be garbage collected — causing a memory leak.

sections.heading: Why It Happens
items: Inner (non-static) AsyncTask classes hold a reference to the outer Activity
The background thread keeps the AsyncTask alive, which keeps the Activity alive
Even after the user rotates the screen, the old Activity stays in memory

sections.heading: How to Prevent (Historical)
items: Use static inner AsyncTask with a WeakReference to the Activity
Cancel the task in onDestroy()
Modern fix: Use coroutines with viewModelScope — automatically cancelled on ViewModel clear

questions.question: UI vs Background Thread
questions.title: UI vs Background Thread

sections.heading: UI Thread (Main Thread)
items: The single thread responsible for all UI rendering and user interaction
Handles touch events, drawing, and view updates
Blocking it causes ANR (Application Not Responding)
Only the UI thread can update the UI

sections.heading: Background Thread
items: Used for heavy work: network calls, database queries, file I/O
Cannot directly update the UI — must post results to the main thread
Created via Thread, Executor, coroutines, WorkManager, etc.

sections.heading: In Short
items: UI thread = renders UI, must stay fast (<16ms per frame)
Background thread = does heavy work off the UI thread
Use runOnUiThread() or Dispatchers.Main to post results back to UI

questions.question: What is ANR? How can it be prevented?
questions.title: What is ANR? How can it be prevented?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ANR (Application Not Responding) occurs when the UI thread is blocked for too long — 5 seconds for user input events, or 10 seconds for BroadcastReceiver. Android shows a dialog letting the user force-close the app.

sections.heading: Common Causes
items: Network or database operations on the main thread
Long-running computations blocking the UI thread
Deadlocks between threads
Heavy work in BroadcastReceiver.onReceive()

sections.heading: How to Prevent
items: Move all heavy work off the main thread using coroutines, WorkManager, or Executor
Use StrictMode in development to detect main-thread violations
Use async APIs for network (Retrofit, OkHttp) and database (Room with suspend functions)
Keep BroadcastReceiver.onReceive() lightweight — offload work to a Service

questions.question: How does Garbage Collection work?
questions.title: How does Garbage Collection work?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Garbage Collection (GC) is the automatic process of reclaiming memory occupied by objects that are no longer reachable by the application.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: The GC starts from root references (stack variables, static fields) and traces reachable objects
Objects that are not reachable from any root are considered garbage
The GC reclaims the memory of unreachable objects
Android uses ART (Android Runtime) which includes a generational GC

sections.heading: Generations in ART
items: Young generation: Newly created objects; collected frequently
Old generation: Long-lived objects; collected less often
Large objects: Stored in a separate space

sections.heading: Key Points
items: GC pauses can cause jank if triggered frequently
Avoid creating excessive short-lived objects in loops
Memory leaks prevent GC from collecting objects — leading to OOM

questions.question: Can you manually call the Garbage Collector?
questions.title: Can you manually call the Garbage Collector?

sections.heading: Answer
sections.content: Yes, you can call System.gc() to suggest that the JVM/ART run the Garbage Collector. However, it is only a hint — the runtime is not obligated to run GC immediately.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: System.gc() is a request, not a command — GC may or may not run
Manually triggering GC can cause unnecessary pauses and jank
ART's GC is well-optimized and runs automatically — manual calls are rarely helpful
Never rely on System.gc() for correctness or memory management

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Fix memory leaks instead of calling GC manually
Profile with Android Studio Memory Profiler to identify issues

questions.question: Memory Leak vs Out of Memory (OOM) Error
questions.title: Memory Leak vs Out of Memory (OOM) Error

sections.heading: Memory Leak
items: Objects are still referenced but no longer needed — GC cannot collect them
Memory usage grows over time without being freed
Does not immediately crash the app but degrades performance
Common causes: static references to Context, unclosed Cursors, inner class references

sections.heading: Out of Memory (OOM)
items: App tries to allocate more memory than the system allows
Results in OutOfMemoryError crash
Often caused by accumulated memory leaks or loading very large bitmaps
Android has per-app heap limits (typically 256MB–512MB)

sections.heading: In Short
items: Memory Leak → gradual memory growth → can eventually cause OOM
OOM → hard crash from exceeding memory limit

questions.question: What is a dangling pointer?
questions.title: What is a dangling pointer?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A dangling pointer is a reference that points to memory that has already been freed or deallocated. Accessing it leads to undefined behavior or crashes.

sections.heading: In Android/Java Context
items: Java and Kotlin don't have raw pointers — the GC manages memory
The equivalent issue is holding a reference to a destroyed object (e.g., a destroyed Activity)
Example: a background thread holds a reference to an Activity that was already finished

sections.heading: In NDK (C/C++) Context
items: Dangling pointers are a real risk when using the Android NDK
Accessing freed memory causes crashes or memory corruption

sections.heading: Prevention
items: Use WeakReference for objects that may be destroyed
Cancel background tasks when the Activity/Fragment is destroyed
Use ViewModel to hold UI data that survives configuration changes

questions.question: Elaborate Memory Leak
questions.title: Elaborate Memory Leak

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A memory leak occurs when an object is no longer needed by the app but is still referenced, preventing the Garbage Collector from freeing its memory. Over time this causes memory usage to grow until the app crashes with OOM.

sections.heading: Common Causes in Android
items: Static reference to Context or Activity — keeps the entire Activity in memory
Anonymous inner classes in Activity (e.g., Runnable, Callback) — hold implicit reference to Activity
Non-cancelled listeners (EventBus, LiveData observers) after Activity destroy
Unclosed resources: Cursor, InputStream, SQLiteDatabase
AsyncTask holding a reference to Activity even after it's destroyed

sections.heading: How to Prevent
items: Use applicationContext instead of Activity context for long-lived objects
Use WeakReference when you must reference an Activity from a background object
Always unregister listeners in onDestroy() or onStop()
Use LeakCanary to detect leaks during development

questions.question: How do you find memory leaks in Android applications?
questions.title: How do you find memory leaks in Android applications?

sections.heading: Tools & Techniques
items: LeakCanary: Automatically detects and reports memory leaks in debug builds — shows the reference chain causing the leak
Android Studio Memory Profiler: Shows heap usage over time; take heap dumps to inspect retained objects
Heap Dump Analysis: Capture heap dump → filter by Activity/Fragment → check for retained instances after destroy
Android Profiler (Allocation Tracker): Track object allocations and see which ones are never released

sections.heading: Manual Detection Signs
items: Memory usage grows with each Activity/Fragment open-close cycle
Multiple instances of the same Activity visible in heap dump
App slows down over time without crashing

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Add LeakCanary as a debugImplementation dependency in every project
Regularly profile memory during development, not just after issues appear

questions.question: How to identify and fix OutOfMemory issues?
questions.title: How to identify and fix OutOfMemory issues?

sections.heading: Identification
items: Check crash logs for OutOfMemoryError and the stack trace
Use Android Studio Memory Profiler to watch heap growth over time
Take heap dumps and look for large retained objects (e.g., large Bitmaps)
Monitor with ActivityManager.getMemoryInfo() at runtime

sections.heading: Common Fixes
items: Bitmaps: Use inSampleSize to downsample large images; use Glide/Coil which handle this automatically
Caching: Use LruCache to cap in-memory cache size
Fix memory leaks: Use LeakCanary, avoid static Activity references
Lazy loading: Don't load all data at once — use Paging library for lists
Avoid large allocations in loops — reuse objects where possible

questions.question: How does the OutOfMemory happen?
questions.title: How does the OutOfMemory happen?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: An OutOfMemoryError is thrown when the JVM/ART cannot allocate enough memory for a new object even after running Garbage Collection.

sections.heading: Common Reasons
items: Loading large uncompressed bitmaps into memory
Accumulated memory leaks filling up the heap over time
Holding too many objects in memory simultaneously (large lists, caches)
Allocating very large arrays or data structures

sections.heading: Android Specifics
items: Each app has a limited heap — dalvik.vm.heapsize (typically 256MB–512MB depending on device)
Apps can request large heap via android:largeHeap=true — but this is not a fix, just a band-aid
OOM can also happen in native memory (NDK allocations)

questions.question: What is the onTrimMemory() method?
questions.title: What is the onTrimMemory() method?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: onTrimMemory() is a callback from the system asking your app to release memory when the device is under memory pressure. It is available in Activity, Fragment, Service, and Application.

sections.heading: Key Levels
items: TRIM_MEMORY_RUNNING_LOW: Device is running low on memory — release non-critical caches
TRIM_MEMORY_UI_HIDDEN: App's UI is no longer visible — release UI-related resources
TRIM_MEMORY_BACKGROUND: App is in background; system may kill it — free as much as possible
TRIM_MEMORY_COMPLETE: App is among the first to be killed — release everything possible

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Clear image caches (Glide/Coil have built-in support for this callback)
Release non-essential in-memory data when notified
Helps your app survive longer in the background

questions.question: Why do we use WeakReferences?
questions.title: Why do we use WeakReferences?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A WeakReference holds a reference to an object without preventing it from being garbage collected. When the GC runs and the object has no strong references, it is collected even if a WeakReference points to it.

sections.heading: Why It's Used
items: Prevents memory leaks when a long-lived object (e.g., a background thread) needs to reference a short-lived one (e.g., an Activity)
The GC can collect the Activity even if the thread still holds a WeakReference to it
Commonly used in Handlers, AsyncTask, and caches

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Always check if weakRef.get() != null before using the reference — it may have been collected
WeakReference vs SoftReference: Soft references are kept until memory is low; weak references are collected at any GC cycle
Modern alternative: Use ViewModel + coroutines to avoid needing WeakReferences altogether

questions.question: Improve Android App Performance
questions.title: Improve Android App Performance

sections.heading: UI Performance
items: Flatten view hierarchies — prefer ConstraintLayout over nested layouts
Use RecyclerView with ViewHolder pattern for lists
Avoid overdraw — reduce overlapping views with transparent backgrounds
Run heavy work off the main thread to maintain 60fps

sections.heading: Memory
items: Fix memory leaks using LeakCanary
Use efficient image loading (Glide/Coil) with caching
Use object pooling and avoid unnecessary allocations in loops

sections.heading: Launch Time
items: Use Baseline Profiles to speed up app startup
Lazy-initialize heavy libraries
Avoid blocking work in Application.onCreate()

sections.heading: Network & Storage
items: Cache responses; use conditional HTTP requests
Use Room for efficient local database queries
Paginate large data sets with Paging 3 library

questions.question: How do you inspect and solve Jank issues?
questions.title: How do you inspect and solve Jank issues?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Jank is visible stuttering in the UI caused by frames taking longer than 16ms to render (60fps target). Each dropped frame causes the animation or scroll to stutter.

sections.heading: How to Inspect
items: Android Studio Profiler → CPU section → Record trace to find slow frames
GPU Rendering profile (Developer Options → Profile GPU rendering): Color bars show frame timing
Systrace / Perfetto: Detailed frame-level tracing to pinpoint slow operations
Choreographer logs: Skipped frames are logged in Logcat

sections.heading: Common Fixes
items: Move heavy computation (sorting, filtering, parsing) off the main thread
Reduce overdraw and flatten view hierarchy
Avoid creating objects during onDraw() or in tight loops
Use DiffUtil in RecyclerView instead of notifyDataSetChanged()
Prefetch data with Paging library to avoid loading stalls

questions.question: Baseline Profiles
questions.title: Baseline Profiles

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Baseline Profiles are a set of critical classes and methods pre-compiled ahead-of-time (AOT) by the Android runtime. They speed up app startup and reduce jank on first launch by avoiding JIT compilation of hot code paths.

sections.heading: How They Work
items: Developer generates a profile using the Macrobenchmark library
Profile is packaged with the app (in assets/dexopt/baseline.prof)
On install, Play Store and ART use it to AOT-compile critical code
Reduces startup time and janky frames on first run

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Introduced in Android 9; fully supported with Jetpack Macrobenchmark
Google reports 40% improvement in startup time for apps using Baseline Profiles
Should be regenerated whenever critical user journeys change

questions.question: What is overdraw?
questions.title: What is overdraw?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Overdraw occurs when the GPU renders the same pixel more than once in a single frame. Each layer of overlapping views that draws to the same pixel wastes GPU time.

sections.heading: How to Detect
items: Enable Debug GPU Overdraw in Developer Options
Colors indicate overdraw level: blue = 1x, green = 2x, pink = 3x, red = 4x+ (bad)
Aim for mostly blue with no large red areas

sections.heading: How to Fix
items: Remove unnecessary backgrounds on views or layouts hidden by other views
Use clipRect() in custom views to skip drawing outside visible bounds
Flatten view hierarchy — fewer layers = less overdraw
Avoid setting a background on the window AND the root layout

questions.question: Why does an Android App lag?
questions.title: Why does an Android App lag?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: App lag (jank/stuttering) occurs when the UI thread cannot complete a frame within 16ms (60fps target). Several factors can cause this.

sections.heading: Common Causes
items: Heavy operations on the main thread (network, DB, large computations)
Excessive or deeply nested view hierarchies causing slow measure/layout passes
High overdraw — GPU renders the same pixels multiple times
Memory pressure triggering frequent GC pauses
Large image loading without caching or downsampling
RecyclerView without DiffUtil causing full redraws on data change
Slow or inefficient database queries blocking the UI thread

sections.heading: Quick Fixes
items: Move all I/O to background threads
Use RecyclerView + DiffUtil for efficient list updates
Profile with Android Studio Profiler to find the exact bottleneck

questions.question: How to use Android Studio Memory Profiler?
questions.title: How to use Android Studio Memory Profiler?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android Studio Memory Profiler is a tool in the Android Profiler that helps you monitor heap usage, detect memory leaks, and analyze object allocations in real time.

sections.heading: How to Use
items: Open: View → Tool Windows → Profiler → Select your app process → Click Memory section
Live heap graph: Watch memory grow/shrink as you navigate the app
Record allocations: Click Record → Perform actions → Stop → View allocated objects and their stack traces
Heap dump: Click Dump Java Heap → Analyze retained objects and find leak suspects
Force GC: Click the GC button to trigger collection before taking a heap dump for cleaner results

sections.heading: What to Look For
items: Memory that grows with each screen open and never decreases — sign of a leak
Multiple instances of the same Activity in a heap dump after navigating away
Large Bitmap objects consuming most of the heap

questions.question: Jetpack DataStore Preferences
questions.title: Jetpack DataStore Preferences

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Jetpack DataStore is a modern data storage solution that replaces SharedPreferences. It stores key-value pairs (Preferences DataStore) or typed objects (Proto DataStore) using Kotlin coroutines and Flow.

sections.heading: Why Use DataStore Over SharedPreferences
items: Asynchronous: Uses coroutines and Flow — no blocking the main thread
Safe: Handles errors via exceptions instead of silently failing
Consistent: No apply()/commit() confusion — writes are atomic
Supports Proto DataStore for strongly-typed storage with Protocol Buffers

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Read via dataStore.data (returns Flow) — observe changes reactively
Write via dataStore.edit {} — suspending function
Backed by a file; thread-safe and process-safe
Preference DataStore: key-value pairs like SharedPreferences but async

questions.question: Persisting data in an Android app
questions.title: Persisting data in an Android app

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Data persistence means saving data so it survives app restarts. Android offers several mechanisms depending on data size and type.

sections.heading: Options
items: SharedPreferences / DataStore: Key-value pairs for small settings and flags
Room Database: Structured relational data using SQLite; supports LiveData and Flow
Internal/External File Storage: Raw files (text, JSON, images) on device storage
SQLite (direct): Low-level database access without an ORM
Network/Cloud: Firebase Firestore, REST APIs for server-backed persistence

sections.heading: Choosing the Right Option
items: Simple flags/settings → DataStore
Structured relational data → Room
Large files (media, documents) → File Storage
User data synced across devices → Cloud/Firebase

questions.question: What is ORM? How does it work?
questions.title: What is ORM? How does it work?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ORM (Object-Relational Mapping) is a technique that maps database tables to objects in code, allowing developers to interact with the database using classes and methods instead of raw SQL queries.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Each database table is mapped to a class (entity)
Each table column maps to a field in the class
The ORM generates SQL queries based on method calls or annotations
CRUD operations are done via DAOs (Data Access Objects) or repository patterns

sections.heading: Android ORM Examples
items: Room: Official Jetpack ORM — compile-time SQL verification, supports LiveData/Flow
Realm: Object-based mobile database with its own storage engine
GreenDAO: Lightweight ORM for SQLite (older, less popular now)

questions.question: What are different ways to store data in your Android app?
questions.title: What are different ways to store data in your Android app?

sections.heading: Storage Options
items: SharedPreferences / DataStore: Key-value pairs for small primitive data (user settings, flags)
Room Database: Local relational database backed by SQLite — best for structured, queryable data
Internal File Storage: Private files stored in the app's internal directory — not accessible by other apps
External File Storage: Files on shared storage (SD card, Downloads) — requires permissions
SQLite (raw): Direct database access without ORM abstraction
Network Storage: REST APIs, Firebase, AWS — for cloud-synced or server-side data
Cache: Temporary data stored in the cache directory — can be cleared by the system

sections.heading: Quick Guide
items: Settings / flags → DataStore
Structured data → Room
Media files → External/Internal File Storage
Cloud sync → Firebase / REST API

questions.question: Explain Scoped Storage in Android
questions.title: Explain Scoped Storage in Android

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Scoped Storage was introduced in Android 10 (Q) and enforced in Android 11 to improve user privacy by restricting how apps access files on external storage.

sections.heading: Key Rules
items: Apps can freely access their own app-specific directories (getExternalFilesDir()) without permissions
To access shared media (photos, videos, audio), use the MediaStore API
To access arbitrary files, use the Storage Access Framework (SAF) — file picker
READ_EXTERNAL_STORAGE and WRITE_EXTERNAL_STORAGE permissions are no longer sufficient for broad file access

sections.heading: Why It Was Introduced
items: Prevent apps from reading other apps' private files
Give users more control over their data
Reduce storage clutter from abandoned app files

questions.question: How to encrypt data in Android?
questions.title: How to encrypt data in Android?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android provides several ways to encrypt sensitive data to protect it from unauthorized access.

sections.heading: Common Approaches
items: EncryptedSharedPreferences (Jetpack Security): Encrypts keys and values transparently using AES-256
EncryptedFile (Jetpack Security): Encrypts files written to disk using AES-256-GCM
Android Keystore System: Stores cryptographic keys securely in hardware-backed storage — keys never leave the secure hardware
SQLCipher: Provides encrypted SQLite database storage

sections.heading: Best Practices
items: Never store encryption keys in SharedPreferences or hardcode them in code
Use Android Keystore to generate and store keys
Use Jetpack Security library for simple encrypted storage needs
Enable full-disk or file-based encryption at the OS level (enabled by default on modern Android)

questions.question: What is commit() and apply() in SharedPreferences?
questions.title: What is commit() and apply() in SharedPreferences?

sections.heading: commit()
items: Writes data synchronously to disk
Blocks the calling thread until the write is complete
Returns true/false to indicate success or failure
Safe to call from a background thread; avoid on main thread

sections.heading: apply()
items: Writes data asynchronously to disk
Returns immediately — does not block the thread
No return value — errors are silently ignored
Preferred for most use cases on the main thread

sections.heading: In Short
items: commit() = synchronous + returns result
apply() = asynchronous + fire and forget
Prefer apply() on the main thread; use commit() when write confirmation is needed

questions.question: How SharedPreferences work internally?
questions.title: How SharedPreferences work internally?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: SharedPreferences stores key-value pairs as an XML file in the app's private internal storage directory (/data/data/package_name/shared_prefs/).

sections.heading: How It Works Internally
items: On first access, the XML file is fully loaded into an in-memory Map
Subsequent reads are served directly from the in-memory cache — very fast
apply() writes to memory immediately, then schedules an async disk write
commit() writes to memory and blocks until the disk write completes
All read/write operations are synchronized using locks for thread safety

sections.heading: Known Limitations
items: The entire file is loaded into memory — not suitable for large data sets
Not safe to use across multiple processes by default
No support for reactive/observable reads — use DataStore instead for that

questions.question: How to implement observable SharedPrefs or observable Databases?
questions.title: How to implement observable SharedPrefs or observable Databases?

sections.heading: Observable SharedPreferences
items: Use SharedPreferences.OnSharedPreferenceChangeListener — register in onStart(), unregister in onStop()
Wrap SharedPreferences in a Flow using callbackFlow{} for coroutine-based observation
Better alternative: Migrate to Jetpack DataStore which natively returns Flow

sections.heading: Observable Databases (Room)
items: Return LiveData<T> from a DAO query — Room automatically notifies observers when data changes
Return Flow<T> from a DAO query — collect in a coroutine scope
Combine with ViewModel and StateFlow for a clean reactive data layer

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Room + Flow is the recommended approach for observable local data in modern Android
DataStore for observable key-value settings
Avoid polling or manual refresh — use reactive streams

questions.question: Can you access your SQLite database for debugging?
questions.title: Can you access your SQLite database for debugging?

sections.heading: Answer
sections.content: Yes, you can access and inspect your app's SQLite database during development using several tools.

sections.heading: Methods
items: Android Studio Database Inspector: Built-in tool (View → Tool Windows → App Inspection → Database Inspector). Shows tables, runs live queries, edits data in real time
ADB Shell: Use adb shell → run-as <package_name> → sqlite3 databases/<db_name>.db to query directly
Pull database file: Use adb pull to copy the .db file to your computer and open with DB Browser for SQLite
Stetho (Facebook): Network-based debugging — opens your DB in Chrome DevTools (older approach)

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Database Inspector in Android Studio is the easiest and most recommended tool for debugging Room or raw SQLite databases

questions.question: What is Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) and why is it used?
questions.title: What is Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) and why is it used?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Write-Ahead Logging (WAL) is a database journaling mode where changes are written to a separate WAL file first, before being applied to the main database file.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Instead of modifying the database directly, changes are appended to a WAL log file
Readers can continue reading the main database while writes happen in the WAL file
WAL is periodically checkpointed — changes are merged back into the main database
On crash recovery, the WAL log is used to ensure consistency

sections.heading: Why It Is Used
items: Better concurrency: Reads and writes can happen simultaneously without blocking each other
Faster writes: Appending to a log is faster than modifying the database in place
Data integrity: Ensures the database is never in a corrupt state on crash
