Room enables WAL mode by default for better performance

questions.question: Why Realm? Realm vs SQLite
questions.title: Why Realm? Realm vs SQLite

sections.heading: Realm
items: Object-based database — stores and retrieves native objects directly (no SQL)
Very fast reads — objects are lazily loaded from memory-mapped files
Built-in reactive support — query results update automatically when data changes
Zero-copy architecture — no serialization/deserialization overhead
Cross-platform (iOS and Android with same schema)

sections.heading: SQLite (via Room)
items: Relational database — uses SQL queries and tables
Requires mapping objects to/from rows (ORM like Room handles this)
Standard on Android — well understood, widely supported
Better for complex relational queries and joins
Room with Flow gives reactive behavior similar to Realm

sections.heading: When to Prefer
items: Realm: When you need fast object storage with minimal boilerplate and cross-platform support
Room/SQLite: For complex relational queries, Android-first apps, and Jetpack ecosystem integration

questions.question: Explain OkHttp Interceptor
questions.title: Explain OkHttp Interceptor

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: An OkHttp Interceptor is a powerful mechanism that allows you to observe, modify, and short-circuit HTTP requests and responses. It works as middleware in the OkHttp call chain.

sections.heading: Types
items: Application Interceptor: Added via addInterceptor() — intercepts between your app and OkHttp. Runs once per call, even for cached responses
Network Interceptor: Added via addNetworkInterceptor() — intercepts between OkHttp and the server. Runs only for actual network requests; can see redirect and retry requests

sections.heading: Common Uses
items: Add authentication headers (Authorization: Bearer token) to every request
Log request and response details (use HttpLoggingInterceptor)
Retry failed requests or handle token refresh
Modify or cache responses

questions.question: OkHttp – HTTP Caching
questions.title: OkHttp – HTTP Caching

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: OkHttp supports HTTP caching to store responses on disk and serve them without hitting the network, improving performance and reducing data usage.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: OkHttp respects standard HTTP cache headers: Cache-Control, ETag, Last-Modified, Expires
Responses are stored in a disk cache (configured via Cache(directory, maxSize))
On subsequent requests, OkHttp checks if a valid cached response exists before making a network call
If the server returns 304 Not Modified, OkHttp serves the cached response

sections.heading: Offline Cache (Force Cache)
items: Use a network interceptor to add Cache-Control: public, max-age=N when online
Use an application interceptor to return Cache-Control: only-if-cached when offline
Allows serving stale responses when no network is available

questions.question: How to enable logging in OkHttp?
questions.title: How to enable logging in OkHttp?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: OkHttp provides HttpLoggingInterceptor to log HTTP request and response details, useful for debugging network calls.

sections.heading: Logging Levels
items: NONE: No logging
BASIC: Logs request method, URL, response code, and size
HEADERS: Logs request and response headers
BODY: Logs headers and full request/response body (most verbose)

sections.heading: Setup
sections.code:
val logging = HttpLoggingInterceptor()
logging.level = HttpLoggingInterceptor.Level.BODY

val client = OkHttpClient.Builder()
    .addInterceptor(logging)
    .build()

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Only use BODY level in debug builds — it logs sensitive data
Use BuildConfig.DEBUG to conditionally enable logging

questions.question: What is Multipart Request in Networking?
questions.title: What is Multipart Request in Networking?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Multipart request is an HTTP request that sends multiple parts of data in a single request, each with its own content type. It is commonly used for uploading files along with other form data.

sections.heading: When It Is Used
items: Uploading a profile photo along with a username
Sending a file (image, PDF, video) to a server
Combining binary data (file) with text fields in one request

sections.heading: In Retrofit
items: Use @Multipart annotation on the API method
Use @Part for individual parts (file as MultipartBody.Part, text as RequestBody)
Content-Type is automatically set to multipart/form-data

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Each part in the request is separated by a boundary string defined in the Content-Type header
Suitable for any combination of binary and text data

questions.question: How does the Android image loading library Glide and Fresco work?
questions.title: How does Glide and Fresco work?

sections.heading: Glide
items: Fetches images from network/disk/resources using a background thread
Decodes and downsamples bitmaps to match the target view size — reduces memory usage
Uses a multi-level cache: memory cache (LruCache) → disk cache → network
Lifecycle-aware: automatically pauses/resumes/clears requests based on Activity/Fragment lifecycle
Supports GIFs, video frames, and custom transformations

sections.heading: Fresco (Facebook)
items: Stores decoded bitmaps in native memory (ashmem) — outside Java heap, reducing GC pressure
Uses a Drawee system for smooth placeholder and loading transitions
Supports progressive JPEG loading — shows a low-res image while full image loads
More complex API than Glide; best suited for apps loading large numbers of images

sections.heading: Prefer
items: Glide for most Android apps — simpler, lifecycle-aware, well-supported
Fresco for image-heavy apps where native memory management is important

questions.question: Internal implementation of Picasso and Glide
questions.title: Internal implementation of Picasso and Glide

sections.heading: Picasso
items: Uses a Dispatcher to manage image requests across a thread pool (ExecutorService)
Two-level cache: memory cache (LruCache) and disk cache (OkHttp disk cache)
Fetches images on background threads; posts results to the main thread via Handler
Uses a BitmapHunter that handles decoding and transformation
Does not automatically downsample to view size — may load full-size bitmaps

sections.heading: Glide
items: Three-level cache: Active resources (current views) → Memory cache (LruCache) → Disk cache → Network
Automatically downsamples bitmaps to match the target view size
Uses BitmapPool to reuse bitmap memory — reduces GC overhead
Engine class coordinates loading, caching, and decoding
Lifecycle integration via RequestManager tied to Activity/Fragment lifecycle

sections.heading: Key Difference
items: Glide auto-resizes bitmaps and pools them for memory efficiency
Picasso loads full-size bitmaps by default — more straightforward but less memory efficient

questions.question: Why do we prefer Retrofit over Volley?
questions.title: Why do we prefer Retrofit over Volley?

sections.heading: Retrofit Advantages
items: Type-safe: Defines API as interfaces with annotations — compile-time checks on request structure
Coroutines support: suspend functions out of the box for clean async code
Flexible converters: GSON, Moshi, Jackson, Protobuf — easy to swap serializers
Built on OkHttp: Leverages connection pooling, interceptors, HTTP/2, and caching
Cleaner code: Less boilerplate compared to Volley's callback-heavy approach

sections.heading: Volley Limitations
items: No built-in coroutine/RxJava support
Callback-based — leads to nested callbacks for complex flows
Less flexible for complex request/response customization
Not ideal for large file uploads/downloads
No official type-safe API contract

sections.heading: When Volley Still Fits
items: Simple small image loading and REST calls with minimal setup
Legacy projects already using Volley

questions.question: GSON vs Jackson vs Moshi
questions.title: GSON vs Jackson vs Moshi

sections.heading: GSON (Google)
items: Simple API; minimal setup
Uses reflection for serialization/deserialization — slower and not R8/ProGuard friendly
Does not support Kotlin null safety well — can deserialize null into non-null types
No code generation; works at runtime

sections.heading: Jackson
items: Extremely feature-rich and highly configurable
Fastest of the three for large payloads
Heavier library — increases APK size
Best suited for server-side Java; overkill for most Android apps

sections.heading: Moshi (Square)
items: Designed for Android/Kotlin — supports Kotlin data classes and null safety natively
Uses code generation (KSP/kapt) for fast, reflection-free parsing
Smaller and more efficient than GSON for Android
Better ProGuard/R8 compatibility

sections.heading: Recommendation
items: Use Moshi for new Android/Kotlin projects
GSON for quick prototypes; Jackson for complex server-side needs

questions.question: How to handle bitmaps in Android as they take too much memory?
questions.title: How to handle bitmaps in Android as they take too much memory?

sections.heading: Key Strategies
items: Downsample with inSampleSize: Use BitmapFactory.Options.inSampleSize to load a scaled-down version of the image instead of the full resolution
Use inJustDecodeBounds: First decode only the dimensions (without allocating pixels) to calculate the required sample size
Reuse bitmaps with BitmapPool: Glide manages a BitmapPool internally to reuse bitmap memory and reduce GC pressure
Use appropriate Bitmap.Config: Use RGB_565 instead of ARGB_8888 to halve memory usage when alpha is not needed
Recycle bitmaps: Call bitmap.recycle() when no longer needed (mostly pre-Honeycomb; ART handles this better now)

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Use image libraries (Glide, Coil) — they handle downsampling, caching, and bitmap recycling automatically
Load bitmaps in background threads only; post results to the UI thread
Never load full-resolution images directly into ImageViews without downsampling

questions.question: Tell about the Bitmap pool
questions.title: Tell about the Bitmap pool

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Bitmap pool is a cache of reusable Bitmap objects. Instead of allocating new Bitmap memory for every image load, the pool provides a previously allocated Bitmap of the same or compatible size, reducing GC overhead.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: When a Bitmap is no longer displayed, it is returned to the pool instead of being GC'd
On the next image load, the pool provides a matching Bitmap; BitmapFactory decodes the new image into it (using inBitmap option)
Glide manages a BitmapPool (backed by LruBitmapPool) internally for all image loads

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Reduces the number of Bitmap allocations — less GC pressure and fewer GC pauses
Improves scrolling performance in RecyclerViews with many images
Memory is reused efficiently without repeated allocation/deallocation

questions.question: How to load large Bitmaps? Handle memory leaks and sample sizing
questions.title: How to load large Bitmaps? Handle memory leaks and sample sizing

sections.heading: Step 1: Decode Dimensions First
items: Set inJustDecodeBounds = true and call BitmapFactory.decodeFile()
This reads only the image dimensions without allocating pixels
Use the dimensions to calculate the required inSampleSize

sections.heading: Step 2: Calculate inSampleSize
items: inSampleSize = 2 means the image is decoded at 1/2 width and 1/2 height (1/4 memory)
Calculate: keep halving until the decoded size is smaller than the target view size
Always use powers of 2 for inSampleSize

sections.heading: Step 3: Decode with inSampleSize
items: Set inJustDecodeBounds = false and decode again with the calculated inSampleSize
The resulting bitmap fits the view size with much lower memory usage

sections.heading: Avoid Memory Leaks
items: Always load bitmaps on background threads; never on the main thread
Recycle bitmaps when the view is destroyed if managing manually
Use Glide/Coil — they handle all of this automatically with lifecycle awareness

questions.question: App Startup Library
questions.title: App Startup Library

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Jetpack App Startup library provides a simple, efficient way to initialize components at app startup. It consolidates multiple ContentProvider-based initializers into a single ContentProvider, reducing startup time.

sections.heading: Problem It Solves
items: Many libraries (WorkManager, Firebase, etc.) use ContentProviders for auto-initialization
Each ContentProvider adds latency to app startup — the more libraries, the slower the launch
App Startup merges all initializers under one ContentProvider (InitializationProvider)

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Implement the Initializer<T> interface for each component you want to initialize
Declare it in the manifest under InitializationProvider
Supports dependency ordering between initializers — initializer A can depend on initializer B
Can be lazy-initialized: call AppInitializer.getInstance(context).initializeComponent() manually

sections.heading: Key Benefits
items: Faster app startup by reducing ContentProvider count
Explicit initialization order and dependency management
Works well with Baseline Profiles for further startup optimization

questions.title: Why use Dependency Injection frameworks like Dagger?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Dependency Injection (DI) is a design pattern where objects receive their dependencies from outside rather than creating them internally. Frameworks like Dagger automate this wiring.

sections.heading: Why Use DI Frameworks
items: Decoupling: Classes don't create their own dependencies — easier to swap implementations
Testability: Inject mock/fake dependencies in tests without changing production code
Reusability: Shared dependencies (e.g., Retrofit, Repository) are created once and reused
Scalability: Manual DI becomes unmanageable in large apps — Dagger generates efficient code at compile time
Lifecycle management: Scoped components ensure correct object lifetimes

sections.heading: Without DI (Problem)
items: Classes are tightly coupled — hard to test and change
Creating objects inside classes violates Single Responsibility Principle

sections.heading: @Inject
items: Marks a constructor, field, or method as a dependency injection point
Dagger uses it to know what to inject and how to create objects

sections.heading: @Module
items: A class that provides dependencies that cannot use @Inject (e.g., interfaces, third-party classes)
Groups related @Provides methods together

sections.heading: @Provides
items: A method inside a @Module that tells Dagger how to create a specific dependency
Used when constructor injection is not possible

sections.heading: @Component
items: An interface that connects @Modules to injection targets
Dagger generates its implementation at compile time
Acts as the bridge between the dependency graph and the consumer

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Dagger is a compile-time DI framework. It processes annotations at build time and generates plain Java code to wire dependencies — no reflection at runtime.

sections.heading: Internal Flow
items: Annotation Processor scans @Component, @Module, @Inject annotations at compile time
Dagger generates Factory classes for each dependency (e.g., MyClass_Factory)
Generates a DaggerMyComponent class implementing your @Component interface
When inject() is called, Dagger calls the generated Factories to create and provide objects
Scope annotations (e.g., @Singleton) generate Provider wrappers that cache instances

sections.heading: Key Point
items: All wiring is resolved at compile time — no reflection, no runtime overhead
Errors in the dependency graph are caught at compile time, not runtime

sections.heading: Dagger 2
items: Full control over component hierarchy and scopes
More boilerplate — manual component setup, subcomponents, and bindings
Better for complex multi-module apps needing fine-grained control
Steeper learning curve

sections.heading: Dagger Hilt
items: Built on top of Dagger 2 — uses it under the hood
Standard components provided by Google: ApplicationComponent, ActivityComponent, ViewModelComponent, etc.
Minimal boilerplate — @HiltAndroidApp, @AndroidEntryPoint, @HiltViewModel
Integrates with Jetpack (ViewModel, WorkManager, Navigation)
Recommended for new Android projects

sections.heading: When to Choose
items: Hilt: Most Android projects — less setup, Jetpack integration, easier testing
Dagger 2: Large projects needing custom component hierarchy or migrating an existing Dagger setup

questions.title: What is a Component in Dagger?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Component in Dagger is an interface annotated with @Component that acts as the dependency graph's entry point. It connects modules (providers) to injection targets (consumers).

sections.heading: Key Responsibilities
items: Declares which modules contribute to the graph
Exposes inject() methods to perform field injection into classes
Can expose dependencies for use in subcomponents or other components
Dagger generates a DaggerXxxComponent implementation at compile time

sections.heading: Scope
items: A Component can be scoped (e.g., @Singleton) to control object lifetimes
Objects scoped to a component live as long as the component instance lives

questions.title: What is a Module in Dagger?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Module is a class annotated with @Module that provides dependencies to the Dagger graph. It is used when constructor injection is not possible — for example, interfaces, third-party classes, or objects requiring complex setup.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Contains @Provides methods that tell Dagger how to create a dependency
Multiple modules can be added to a single Component
@Binds can be used instead of @Provides to bind an interface to its implementation with less overhead
Modules are included in a @Component via the modules parameter

questions.title: How does custom scope work in Dagger?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Scopes in Dagger control the lifetime of objects. A scoped binding means only one instance of that object is created per component instance.

sections.heading: How to Create a Custom Scope
items: Define a custom annotation with @Scope and @Retention(RUNTIME)
Apply the scope annotation to both the @Component and the @Provides/@Inject method
Dagger generates a scoped provider that caches the instance for the component's lifetime

sections.heading: Example
items: @ActivityScope: One instance per Activity — applied to ActivityComponent
@UserScope: One instance per logged-in user session
@Singleton: One instance per ApplicationComponent (app lifetime)

sections.heading: Key Point
items: The scope does not define the lifetime — the component does
Destroying the component destroys all scoped objects within it

sections.heading: Component
items: A standalone dependency graph
Access to parent component's bindings requires explicit component dependency declaration
Two components with component dependency cannot share the same scope
Used when components are loosely related

sections.heading: SubComponent
items: A child component nested inside a parent component
Automatically inherits all bindings from the parent component
Can access all parent bindings without explicit exposure
Tighter coupling — declared inside the parent module or component
Used when parent-child relationship is clear (e.g., AppComponent → ActivitySubComponent)

sections.heading: In Short
items: SubComponent: inherits everything from parent automatically
Component dependency: only gets what the parent explicitly exposes

questions.title: Circular dependency in Dagger — how to resolve?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A circular dependency occurs when class A depends on class B, and class B depends on class A — forming an infinite creation loop. Dagger will fail to compile when it detects this.

sections.heading: How to Resolve
items: Introduce a Provider<T>: Inject Provider<B> in class A instead of B directly — defers creation and breaks the cycle
Extract shared state: Move the common dependency into a third class C that both A and B depend on
Refactor design: Usually circular dependency indicates poor separation of concerns — rethink responsibilities
Use @Lazy<T>: Lazily initializes the dependency on first access, breaking the instantiation cycle

sections.heading: @Provides
items: A method inside a @Module that creates and returns a dependency instance
Can contain custom logic, configuration, or third-party setup
The method body is executed by Dagger
Used when you need to construct the object yourself

sections.heading: @Binds
items: An abstract method that binds an interface to its implementation
No method body — Dagger generates the binding directly
More efficient: generates less code than @Provides
Used when the implementation already has @Inject constructor — just tells Dagger which interface maps to which class

sections.heading: In Short
items: @Provides = create the object yourself (with logic)
@Binds = tell Dagger to use this existing class for an interface (no logic)

questions.title: SubComponent vs Component dependency — under the hood

sections.heading: SubComponent (under the hood)
items: SubComponent shares the same object graph scope as parent
Generated as an inner class of the parent component implementation
Has direct access to all parent bindings — no interface exposure needed
Lifetime is explicitly controlled: created and destroyed by the parent component

sections.heading: Component Dependency (under the hood)
items: Two independent components — each has its own scope and object graph
Parent must explicitly expose bindings in its interface for the child to use
Child component holds a reference to the parent component interface
More flexible — components can be mixed from different modules or libraries

sections.heading: Key Difference
items: SubComponent: tight integration, shared graph, inner class generation
Component dependency: loose coupling, explicit exposure, separate graphs

questions.title: Constructor injection vs method injection. Scope and Singleton

sections.heading: Constructor Injection
items: Dependencies are provided via the constructor
Preferred approach — makes dependencies explicit and immutable
Works with @Inject on the constructor — Dagger knows how to build the class
Best for testability: easy to pass mocks in tests

sections.heading: Method (Field) Injection
items: Dependencies are injected into fields or methods after object creation
Used when you don't control the constructor (e.g., Activity, Fragment, Service)
Annotate fields with @Inject; call component.inject(this)
Less testable than constructor injection

sections.heading: Scope and @Singleton
items: @Singleton is a built-in Dagger scope — one instance per component
Applied to both the @Component and the @Provides/@Inject class
The instance lives as long as the component lives (app lifetime for ApplicationComponent)
Custom scopes work the same way with custom scope annotations

sections.heading: What Makes Hilt Interesting
items: Standardized components: Predefined components (ApplicationComponent, ActivityComponent, ViewModelComponent) eliminate boilerplate component setup
@HiltViewModel: Directly injects into ViewModels — no ViewModelFactory boilerplate needed
@AndroidEntryPoint: Single annotation on Activity/Fragment enables injection
Testing: Replaces components with test doubles via @UninstallModules and @BindValue
Gradle plugin: Transforms bytecode to avoid writing boilerplate injection code
Integrates with all Jetpack libraries: WorkManager, Navigation, Compose

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Hilt is opinionated — you trade flexibility for convention and speed of development
For most Android apps it is the right choice

sections.heading: Dagger (+ Hilt)
items: Compile-time code generation — errors caught at build time
Zero runtime overhead — generated code is plain Java/Kotlin
Better performance for large apps
Steeper learning curve; more boilerplate (less with Hilt)
Industry standard in large Android teams

sections.heading: Koin
items: Pure Kotlin DSL — no annotation processing, no code generation
Runtime dependency resolution — errors appear at runtime, not compile time
Very easy to set up and learn — few lines to configure
Small overhead at runtime due to reflection-free service locator pattern
Good for small to medium apps or teams preferring simplicity

sections.heading: When to Choose
items: Dagger/Hilt: Large apps, teams, performance-critical, compile-time safety needed
Koin: Rapid development, small teams, Kotlin-first projects, startup speed matters

sections.heading: Overview
sections.content: Koin is a lightweight DI framework written in pure Kotlin using a DSL. It acts as a service locator under the hood rather than true compile-time DI.

sections.heading: Pros
items: Very easy to set up — no kapt/KSP annotation processing required
Readable Kotlin DSL: single(), factory(), viewModel() declarations
Fast onboarding for new developers
Works well with Kotlin Multiplatform (KMP)

sections.heading: Cons
items: Runtime resolution: dependency errors surface at runtime (on first inject), not at compile time
Service locator pattern: dependencies are not as explicit as constructor injection
Slower than Dagger for very large graphs due to runtime lookups

sections.heading: Verdict
items: Great for smaller projects or teams wanting simplicity; Hilt/Dagger preferred for production-grade large apps

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: RxJava is a Java implementation of Reactive Extensions (ReactiveX). It enables composing asynchronous and event-based programs using observable sequences and functional operators.

sections.heading: Core Concepts
items: Observable: Emits a stream of data or events
Observer: Subscribes to the Observable and reacts to emitted items
Operators: Transform, filter, combine streams (map, flatMap, filter, zip, etc.)
Schedulers: Control which thread work runs on (IO, Computation, Main thread)
Disposable: Manages subscription lifecycle — call dispose() to stop

sections.heading: Key Benefits
items: Composable async code — no callback hell
Thread management via subscribeOn/observeOn
Rich set of operators for transforming data streams
Handles errors in a unified way via onError()

questions.title: How to handle errors in RxJava?

sections.heading: Error Handling Operators
items: onErrorReturn(fn): Emits a fallback item when an error occurs — stream completes normally
onErrorResumeNext(fn): Switches to a different Observable on error — continues the stream
retry(n): Resubscribes to the source Observable up to n times on error
retryWhen(fn): Retries based on a condition — useful for exponential backoff
doOnError(fn): Side-effect only — logs or reports the error without modifying the stream

sections.heading: In subscriber
items: The onError() callback in subscribe{} is the final handler for unhandled errors
Always provide an onError handler — unhandled errors crash the app with UndeliverableException

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Prefer onErrorReturn or onErrorResumeNext to keep the stream alive
Use retry/retryWhen for transient failures like network errors

sections.heading: map()
items: Transforms each emitted item synchronously into another item
Returns the transformed item directly (not an Observable)
Use when the transformation is simple and doesn't involve async work
Example: map { user -> user.name }

sections.heading: flatMap()
items: Transforms each item into an Observable, then merges the results
Used when the transformation itself is asynchronous (e.g., a network call)
Results may interleave — order is not guaranteed
Example: flatMap { userId -> fetchUserFromNetwork(userId) }

sections.heading: In Short
items: map = synchronous 1:1 transform
flatMap = async 1:Observable transform, results merged (unordered)
Use concatMap instead of flatMap if order must be preserved

sections.heading: Observable.create()
items: Gives full control — you manually call emitter.onNext(), onError(), onComplete()
Used for wrapping custom async APIs or callback-based code
Must handle backpressure and disposal manually
More boilerplate; risk of misuse (e.g., forgetting to check isDisposed)

sections.heading: Observable.fromCallable()
items: Wraps a single synchronous computation that returns one value or throws
Automatically handles onNext + onComplete after the call returns
Automatically propagates exceptions as onError
Simpler and safer for wrapping blocking or single-result operations

sections.heading: In Short
items: fromCallable = simple single-value sync operation (prefer this)
create = complex async/multi-emission scenarios needing manual control

questions.title: When to use defer operator in RxJava?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Observable.defer() creates a new Observable for each subscriber. The Observable factory function is called fresh at subscription time, not at declaration time.

sections.heading: When to Use It
items: When the Observable depends on a value that may change between subscriptions (e.g., a mutable variable or state)
Without defer, the Observable captures the variable's value at creation time — stale data
With defer, the Observable is re-created on each subscribe — always reads current value

sections.heading: Example Use Case
items: A flag that controls which API endpoint to hit — defer re-evaluates the flag on each subscription
Lazy initialization of an Observable that reads from a changing source

questions.title: Timer, Delay, and Interval operators in RxJava

sections.heading: Observable.timer()
items: Emits a single item (0L) after a specified delay
Use for one-shot delayed execution
Example: Observable.timer(5, TimeUnit.SECONDS) — fires once after 5 seconds

sections.heading: Observable.interval()
items: Emits incrementing Long values at a fixed interval indefinitely
Use for polling or repeating periodic tasks
Example: Observable.interval(1, TimeUnit.SECONDS) — fires every second

sections.heading: delay()
items: Shifts the emission of all items from an existing Observable by a time delay
Does not delay subscription — delays the delivery of items
Example: observable.delay(2, TimeUnit.SECONDS) — all emissions arrive 2 seconds later

questions.title: Two network calls in parallel using RxJava

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Use zip() or combineLatest() to run two network calls in parallel and combine their results.

sections.heading: Using zip()
items: zip() subscribes to both Observables simultaneously
Waits for both to emit, then combines the results with a zipper function
Emits a single combined result when both complete
Both calls are subscribed on Schedulers.io() for parallel execution

sections.heading: Example
Key Point
items: zip() waits for both; combineLatest() re-emits whenever either source emits

sections.heading: merge()
items: Subscribes to all Observables simultaneously (parallel)
Emits items as they arrive — interleaved order
Does not wait for one to complete before subscribing to the next
Use when order doesn't matter and you want results as fast as possible

sections.heading: concat()
items: Subscribes to Observables sequentially — waits for first to complete before subscribing to next
Preserves order of emissions
Use when order matters or second call depends on first completing
Example: Load from cache first, then network

sections.heading: In Short
items: merge = parallel, unordered
concat = sequential, ordered

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Subject in RxJava acts as both an Observable and an Observer. It can receive items (via onNext) and emit them to subscribers simultaneously — a bridge between imperative and reactive code.

sections.heading: Types of Subjects
items: PublishSubject: Emits only items emitted after subscription — new subscribers miss past items
BehaviorSubject: Emits the most recent item to new subscribers, then continues with subsequent items
ReplaySubject: Emits all past items to any new subscriber (configurable buffer size)
AsyncSubject: Only emits the last item once the source completes

sections.heading: Common Use Case
items: Act as an event bus: call subject.onNext(event) to broadcast to all subscribers
Bridge callback APIs to reactive streams

sections.heading: Observable
items: Emits 0 to N items, followed by onComplete or onError
Does not handle backpressure — use Flowable if consumer is slower than producer

sections.heading: Flowable
items: Like Observable but with backpressure support
Use when emitting large or infinite streams where consumer may be slower

sections.heading: Single
items: Emits exactly one item or an error — like a Future
Use for network calls or database queries returning one result

sections.heading: Maybe
items: Emits 0 or 1 items, or an error — optional single result
Use when result may or may not be present

sections.heading: Completable
items: Emits no items — only signals completion or error
Use for fire-and-forget operations (write to DB, send analytics)

sections.heading: Observable
items: Emits 0 to N items over time
Suitable for streams (list of items, events, polling results)
onNext + onError + onComplete callbacks

sections.heading: Single
items: Emits exactly 1 item or an error
Suitable for single-value async operations: network call, DB query
onSuccess + onError callbacks

sections.heading: Completable
items: Emits no items — only signals done or error
Suitable for write operations, DELETE, POST with no response body
onComplete + onError callbacks

sections.heading: Quick Guide
items: List of items → Observable
One result → Single
No result → Completable
Optional result → Maybe

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Use RxJava operators on a search EditText to implement efficient, debounced search that avoids triggering a network call on every keystroke.

sections.heading: Key Operators
items: debounce(300, MILLISECONDS): Waits 300ms after the user stops typing before emitting — avoids excessive API calls
distinctUntilChanged(): Skips emission if the query is the same as the previous — no duplicate calls
filter { it.length >= 3 }: Only search when query is at least 3 characters
switchMap: Cancels the previous search Observable and subscribes to the new one — only latest result matters

sections.heading: Example
Schedulers.io()
items: Backed by an unbounded thread pool — grows as needed
Optimized for I/O-bound work: network calls, file reads, database queries
Threads spend time waiting (blocking) — more threads are needed
Use for: Retrofit calls, Room queries, file I/O

sections.heading: Schedulers.computation()
items: Backed by a fixed thread pool — number of threads = number of CPU cores
Optimized for CPU-bound work: calculations, transformations, parsing
Threads should not block — keep them busy computing
Use for: JSON parsing, image processing, sorting large lists

sections.heading: In Short
items: io() = blocking I/O work (thread pool grows)
computation() = CPU-bound work (fixed pool = core count)

sections.heading: clear()
items: Disposes all currently added disposables
The CompositeDisposable itself remains usable — new disposables can be added after clear()
Use when you want to cancel all subscriptions but reuse the container (e.g., on ViewModel re-subscription)

sections.heading: dispose()
items: Disposes all disposables AND marks the CompositeDisposable itself as disposed
Any new disposables added after dispose() are immediately disposed
The container is effectively dead — cannot be reused
Use in onDestroy() or onCleared() when the component is permanently destroyed

sections.heading: In Short
items: clear() = cancel all, keep the container reusable
dispose() = cancel all, destroy the container permanently

sections.heading: subscribeOn()
items: Specifies the Scheduler on which the Observable source runs (subscription and emission)
Only the first subscribeOn() in the chain has effect — subsequent ones are ignored
Use once to define where the heavy work happens (e.g., Schedulers.io())

sections.heading: observeOn()
items: Specifies the Scheduler on which downstream operators and the final Observer run
Can be used multiple times in a chain — each changes the thread for operators below it
Use to switch to the main thread for UI updates: observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread())

sections.heading: Typical Pattern
items: subscribeOn(Schedulers.io()) — run network/DB work on IO thread
observeOn(AndroidSchedulers.mainThread()) — deliver results on UI thread

sections.heading: Observer (for Observable)
items: Used with Observable.subscribe()
Callbacks: onNext(), onError(), onComplete()
No backpressure support — use with Observable, Single, Maybe, Completable

sections.heading: Subscriber (for Flowable)
items: Used with Flowable.subscribe()
Implements Subscription — supports backpressure via request(n)
Must call subscription.request(Long.MAX_VALUE) or a specific count to pull items
Use with Flowable when the producer may be faster than the consumer

sections.heading: In Short
items: Observer = no backpressure, used with Observable
Subscriber = backpressure aware, used with Flowable

sections.heading: Schedulers.io()
items: Uses a cached thread pool — reuses idle threads before creating new ones
More efficient for many short-lived tasks
Thread pool grows as needed but recycles idle threads after 60 seconds
Use for I/O-bound tasks

sections.heading: Schedulers.newThread()
items: Creates a brand new thread for every subscription — no thread reuse
More overhead than Schedulers.io() — each call spins up a new OS thread
Rarely the right choice; prefer io() for I/O work
Use only when you explicitly need a dedicated isolated thread per task

questions.title: RxJava vs Coroutines — which do you prefer?

sections.heading: RxJava
items: Rich operator library for complex stream transformations (zip, combineLatest, window, buffer, etc.)
Excellent for event streams and multi-value sequences
Mature ecosystem with wide adoption
More verbose; steeper learning curve
Heavy library; adds APK size

sections.heading: Kotlin Coroutines + Flow
items: Native Kotlin — cleaner, more readable sequential-style async code
Flow for streams; suspend functions for single values
Structured concurrency — auto-cancellation tied to lifecycles (viewModelScope, lifecycleScope)
First-class Jetpack support; preferred by Google for new Android projects
Lighter weight; no additional dependency needed

sections.heading: Preference
items: Coroutines + Flow for new projects — better Kotlin integration, structured concurrency, simpler code
RxJava when working with existing RxJava codebase or needing specific complex stream operators

sections.heading: Observable.just(item)
items: Emits the provided items (up to 10) and completes
Items are passed at creation time — not deferred
Use for emitting a fixed set of known items

sections.heading: Observable.fromIterable / fromArray
items: Converts a collection or array into an Observable
Emits each element in order and completes
Use for iterating over an existing list or array

sections.heading: Observable.create()
items: Full control — you drive emissions manually via ObservableEmitter
Used for wrapping callbacks, custom async APIs, multi-emission sources
Most flexible but most complex; handle disposal and errors manually

sections.heading: In Short
items: just = fixed items
from = collection/array
create = custom/manual emission logic

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin Flow is a cold asynchronous stream that emits multiple values sequentially. Built on coroutines, it is the Kotlin-native replacement for RxJava Observables.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Cold stream: Code in the flow builder runs only when collected — not on creation
Backpressure handled by design via coroutine suspension — no explicit backpressure operators needed
Built-in cancellation: Collection is cancelled when the coroutine scope is cancelled
Operators: map, filter, transform, zip, combine, flatMapLatest, debounce, and more

sections.heading: Types
items: Flow: Cold stream — emits on each collection
StateFlow: Hot stream — always holds a current value; replaces LiveData in ViewModel
SharedFlow: Hot stream — for broadcasting events to multiple collectors; configurable replay

sections.heading: val
items: Read-only property — cannot be reassigned after initialization
Value is determined at runtime
Can be used as instance property, local variable, or top-level property
Getter can be overridden — value can be computed dynamically

sections.heading: const val
items: Compile-time constant — value is inlined at every usage site by the compiler
Must be a primitive type or String
Must be a top-level property or inside an object/companion object
Cannot have a custom getter — value must be known at compile time

sections.heading: In Short
items: val = runtime immutable reference
const val = compile-time constant, inlined by compiler

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: These are lifecycle-aware coroutine launchers from lifecycleScope that suspend execution when the lifecycle falls below a certain state. They are now deprecated in favor of repeatOnLifecycle.

sections.heading: Behavior
items: launchWhenCreated: Starts when CREATED; suspends when below CREATED (rarely useful)
launchWhenStarted: Starts when STARTED; suspends (not cancels) when below STARTED
launchWhenResumed: Starts when RESUMED; suspends when below RESUMED

sections.heading: Why They Are Deprecated
items: They suspend the coroutine but don't cancel upstream flows — the producer keeps running even when UI is gone, wasting resources
repeatOnLifecycle(State.STARTED) is the correct replacement — it cancels and restarts the coroutine on lifecycle transitions, stopping the upstream producer when not needed

questions.title: Describe the architecture of your last app

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A well-structured answer follows Clean Architecture layered with MVVM pattern.

sections.heading: Typical Architecture
items: Presentation Layer: Activities/Fragments + ViewModels (MVVM). ViewModel holds UI state as StateFlow/LiveData
Domain Layer: Use cases (interactors) containing business logic. Pure Kotlin — no Android dependencies
Data Layer: Repositories coordinating between remote (Retrofit) and local (Room) data sources
DI: Hilt for dependency injection across all layers
Navigation: Jetpack Navigation Component with a single Activity

sections.heading: Key Principles
items: Unidirectional Data Flow: UI observes ViewModel state; user events flow up to ViewModel
Separation of Concerns: Each layer has a single responsibility
Testability: Domain and Data layers have no Android dependencies — easily unit tested

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel) is an architecture pattern that separates UI logic from business logic using a ViewModel as a mediator.

sections.heading: Layers
items: Model: Data layer — repositories, data sources, network/DB models
View: UI layer — Activities, Fragments. Observes ViewModel state; forwards user events to ViewModel
ViewModel: Holds and manages UI state. Processes user events and fetches data from the Model. Survives configuration changes

sections.heading: Data Flow
items: View observes LiveData/StateFlow from ViewModel
User interaction → View calls ViewModel function → ViewModel updates state → View reflects new state

sections.heading: Key Benefits
items: ViewModel survives rotation — no data loss
Separation of concerns — UI and business logic are decoupled
Easily testable ViewModel with no UI dependencies

sections.heading: MVC (Model-View-Controller)
items: Controller handles user input and updates Model and View
In Android, Activity acts as both View and Controller — violates separation of concerns
Tight coupling between View and Controller; hard to test

sections.heading: MVP (Model-View-Presenter)
items: Presenter handles all UI logic; View is passive (only renders)
View and Presenter communicate via interfaces — easier to test Presenter
Problem: Presenter holds a reference to View — risk of memory leaks if not detached

sections.heading: MVVM (Model-View-ViewModel)
items: ViewModel exposes observable state; View binds to it
No direct reference from ViewModel to View — no memory leak risk
ViewModel survives configuration changes
Supported natively by Jetpack — the recommended pattern for Android

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Clean Architecture (by Robert C. Martin) organizes code in concentric layers where dependencies only point inward — inner layers know nothing about outer layers.

sections.heading: Layers (outer to inner)
items: Frameworks & Drivers: Android framework, Retrofit, Room, UI (outermost)
Interface Adapters: ViewModels, Presenters, Controllers, Mappers
Application/Domain: Use Cases (interactors) — business rules
Entities: Core business models — no Android or framework dependencies (innermost)

sections.heading: Dependency Rule
items: Dependencies always point inward — Domain knows nothing about Retrofit or Room
Data and Presentation layers implement interfaces defined in Domain
This makes the Domain layer fully testable with plain JUnit — no mocks needed for Android

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Highly testable, maintainable, and scalable
Swappable implementations: replace Retrofit with another HTTP client without touching business logic

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: MVI (Model-View-Intent) is a unidirectional architecture where the View sends Intents (user actions) to the ViewModel, which processes them and emits a new immutable State back to the View.

sections.heading: Key Concepts
items: Intent: User action or UI event (not Android Intent) — e.g., ButtonClicked, RefreshTriggered
State: Immutable data class representing the complete UI state at a given moment
Model: Processes intents and produces new state (ViewModel in Android implementation)
Single source of truth: One state object describes the entire screen — no partial updates

sections.heading: Data Flow
items: User action → Intent → ViewModel processes → emits new State → View renders state

sections.heading: vs MVVM
items: MVI has strict unidirectional flow and immutable state — eliminates state inconsistency bugs
More boilerplate than MVVM; better for complex screens with many interaction states

sections.heading: Software Architecture
items: High-level structure of a system — how major components are organized and interact
Concerned with: layering (Clean Architecture), patterns (MVVM, MVI), module boundaries, scalability
Hard to change once established — foundational decisions
Example: Deciding to use Clean Architecture with MVVM and a multi-module setup

sections.heading: Software Design
items: Low-level implementation details within components — how individual classes and functions are structured
Concerned with: SOLID principles, design patterns (Factory, Observer, Builder), class interactions
Easier to refactor than architecture
Example: Deciding to use a Builder pattern for constructing a complex object

sections.heading: In Short
items: Architecture = structure of the system (macro)
Design = structure of the code within the system (micro)

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Faster build times: Gradle only recompiles modules that changed — incremental builds
Clear separation of concerns: Module boundaries enforce layer and feature isolation
Reusability: Shared modules (e.g., :core, :network) are reused across features
Parallel development: Teams can work on separate modules without conflicts
Dynamic delivery: Feature modules can be delivered on demand via Play Feature Delivery
Scalability: Each module is independently versioned and testable

sections.heading: Why
items: Build performance: Only modified modules recompile — Gradle caching is more effective
Enforced architecture: Module visibility rules prevent layer violations at compile time
Testability: Each module is independently testable
Dynamic Feature Delivery: Feature modules can be downloaded on demand

sections.heading: When to Use
items: Large apps with multiple features developed by different teams
When build time of a monolithic module becomes unacceptable
When features need to be delivered dynamically via Play Store
When strict API boundaries between layers/features are required

sections.heading: When NOT to Use
items: Small apps or MVPs — overhead of module setup outweighs benefits
When team is small and all working in the same feature area

sections.heading: S — Single Responsibility
items: A class should have only one reason to change — do one thing well
Example: ViewModel handles UI state; Repository handles data fetching — not both in one class

sections.heading: O — Open/Closed
items: Open for extension, closed for modification
Add new behavior via new classes/functions, not by changing existing code

sections.heading: L — Liskov Substitution
items: Subclasses must be substitutable for their parent class without breaking behavior

sections.heading: I — Interface Segregation
items: Many small, specific interfaces are better than one large general interface
Clients should not be forced to depend on methods they don't use

sections.heading: D — Dependency Inversion
items: High-level modules should not depend on low-level modules — both should depend on abstractions
Example: ViewModel depends on a Repository interface, not a concrete implementation

questions.title: MVP vs MVVM — issues with Presenter

sections.heading: Issues with Presenter in MVP
items: Presenter holds a direct reference to the View interface — if Activity is destroyed, the Presenter still holds it → memory leak
Must manually detach/attach View in onResume/onDestroy — boilerplate
Does not survive configuration changes — Presenter must be recreated or saved manually
Testing requires mocking the View interface

sections.heading: How MVVM Solves These
items: ViewModel has no reference to View — observes via LiveData/StateFlow — no memory leak
ViewModel survives configuration changes (rotation) by design
No manual attach/detach needed
ViewModel is easily tested without mocking UI

sections.heading: Summary
items: MVP: More explicit contract (interfaces) but lifecycle and memory issues
MVVM: Lifecycle-safe, configuration-change-safe, less boilerplate with Jetpack

sections.heading: MVVM
items: State can be multiple LiveData/StateFlow fields — partial updates possible
Bidirectional data binding possible (can lead to state inconsistency)
Less boilerplate — simple screens are easy to implement
ViewModel functions can be called directly from View

sections.heading: MVI
items: Single immutable State object — no partial update inconsistency
Strictly unidirectional: Intent → ViewModel → State → View
More boilerplate: sealed class for Intents, data class for State, side effects channel
Better for complex screens with many possible states and transitions

sections.heading: Choose
items: MVVM: Most Android apps — simpler, Jetpack-native
MVI: Complex screens with many states, teams needing strict predictability

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Clean Architecture and MVVM are complementary — MVVM defines the presentation pattern while Clean Architecture defines the overall layering.

sections.heading: Layer Mapping
items: Presentation: ViewModel (MVVM) + UI (Activity/Fragment/Compose) — observes state, sends events
Domain: Use Cases containing business logic — pure Kotlin, no Android dependencies
Data: Repositories + Data Sources (Retrofit, Room) — implements Domain interfaces

sections.heading: How They Work Together
items: ViewModel calls a Use Case from the Domain layer
Use Case calls a Repository interface (defined in Domain, implemented in Data)
Repository fetches from network or DB and returns domain models
ViewModel maps to UI state and updates StateFlow/LiveData

sections.heading: Benefits
items: Fully testable at each layer independently
Business logic is Android-free — tested with plain JUnit

sections.heading: Creational Patterns
items: Singleton: One instance of Retrofit/OkHttp client across the app
Builder: Building OkHttpClient, Notification, AlertDialog with optional parameters
Factory: Creating different View types in RecyclerView adapter (getItemViewType)

sections.heading: Structural Patterns
items: Adapter: RecyclerView.Adapter, Retrofit converter adapters
Decorator: OkHttp Interceptors add behavior to requests without changing OkHttp itself
Facade: Repository pattern hides the complexity of multiple data sources

sections.heading: Behavioral Patterns
items: Observer: LiveData, Flow, RxJava — UI observes ViewModel state
Strategy: Swapping sorting or filtering algorithms at runtime
Command: ViewModel intents in MVI architecture

sections.heading: Builder Pattern
items: Constructs a complex object step by step with optional parameters
The client controls what gets set
Example: OkHttpClient.Builder().connectTimeout(30, SECONDS).addInterceptor(logging).build()
Use when: Object has many optional parameters and you want readable construction

sections.heading: Factory Pattern
items: Creates objects without exposing creation logic — the factory decides which class to instantiate
Client only knows the product interface, not the concrete class
Example: ViewModelProvider.Factory creates specific ViewModel instances
Use when: The type of object to create depends on input or configuration

sections.heading: Key Difference
items: Builder = constructs one object with varying configuration (same type)
Factory = creates different types of objects based on input (polymorphism)

questions.title: Singleton vs Static — why we use them

sections.heading: Singleton
items: A class with only one instance per application lifetime
Instance is stored on the heap — has state, can implement interfaces, can be injected
Lazy or eager initialization — controllable
Example: A database connection, Retrofit instance
Testable: Can be replaced via DI with a mock

sections.heading: Static
items: Belongs to the class itself, not an instance — stored in the class's memory area
No instantiation needed — accessed via ClassName.method()
Cannot implement interfaces or be easily mocked in tests
Example: Math.max(), utility/helper methods with no state

sections.heading: In Short
items: Singleton = one managed instance on the heap — stateful, injectable, testable
Static = class-level method/field — stateless utilities, not injectable or mockable

questions.title: Singleton pattern — problems and double-checked locking

sections.heading: Problems with Singleton
items: Global state: Makes code hard to test — every test shares the same instance
Thread safety: In multi-threaded environments, multiple threads may create multiple instances simultaneously if not synchronized
Hidden dependencies: Components secretly depend on the Singleton — hard to trace

sections.heading: Double-Checked Locking
items: A pattern to lazily create a thread-safe Singleton without full synchronization on every access
Check if null → synchronize → check again → create if still null
The volatile keyword prevents instruction reordering by the JVM

sections.heading: Kotlin Example
Kotlin Alternative
items: Use object declaration — Kotlin guarantees thread-safe lazy initialization via class loading

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A Spannable is an interface in Android for text that can have markup objects (spans) attached to ranges of characters or paragraphs. It allows different styles or behaviors to be applied to parts of a string.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Spans can change appearance (bold, color, size, underline) or behavior (clickable, URL)
Spannable is the mutable interface; Spanned is read-only
SpannableString and SpannableStringBuilder are the common implementations
Used with TextView to display richly formatted text without HTML

sections.heading: Common Span Types
items: ForegroundColorSpan: Changes text color
StyleSpan: Bold or Italic
ClickableSpan: Makes text clickable
URLSpan: Turns text into a hyperlink

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: SpannableString is an implementation of Spannable that wraps an immutable character sequence but allows spans (markup) to be attached to character ranges.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: Create a SpannableString from a String
Use setSpan(span, start, end, flags) to attach a span to a range
Pass it to TextView.setText()
Flags control how the span behaves when text is inserted (e.g., SPAN_EXCLUSIVE_EXCLUSIVE)

sections.heading: Example
sections.content:
val text = SpannableString("Hello World")
text.setSpan(ForegroundColorSpan(Color.RED), 0, 5, Spanned.SPAN_EXCLUSIVE_EXCLUSIVE)
textView.text = text

sections.heading: SpannableString vs SpannableStringBuilder
items: SpannableString: Fixed character content — cannot change the text itself
SpannableStringBuilder: Mutable — can insert, delete, and add spans dynamically

questions.title: Best practices for using text in Android

sections.heading: Best Practices
items: Always store strings in res/values/strings.xml — never hardcode in code or layouts
Use string resources for localization (i18n) support
Use string formatting with getString(R.string.key, arg) for dynamic values
Use sp (scale-independent pixels) for text sizes — respects user font size settings
Avoid setting text color hardcoded in code — use color resources or theme attributes
Use Spannable for rich text instead of HTML string concatenation
Use TextView autoSizeTextType for text that should fit its container
Use TextUtils.isEmpty() to check for null or empty strings safely

sections.heading: Steps
items: Use Theme.MaterialComponents.DayNight as the base theme in styles.xml
Define color resources in res/values/colors.xml for light and res/values-night/colors.xml for dark
Use ?attr/colorPrimary and theme attributes in layouts instead of hardcoded colors
Use AppCompatDelegate.setDefaultNightMode() to programmatically switch modes
Persist the user's choice in DataStore/SharedPreferences and apply on app start

sections.heading: Drawable/Image Support
items: Add dark variants in res/drawable-night/ folder
Use vector drawables that adapt to tint from theme attributes

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Never hardcode colors in layouts or code — always use theme attributes so dark mode works automatically

questions.title: Process of creating a custom view

sections.heading: Steps
items: Extend View (or a subclass like ImageView/LinearLayout)
Override constructors — support all 3 (Context, AttributeSet, defStyleAttr)
Declare custom attributes in res/values/attrs.xml with <declare-styleable>
Parse custom attributes in init{} using context.obtainStyledAttributes()
Override onMeasure(): Measure the view's desired size and call setMeasuredDimension()
Override onLayout(): Position child views (for ViewGroup only)
Override onDraw(): Draw custom graphics using Canvas and Paint
Invalidate with invalidate() to trigger a redraw; requestLayout() to trigger remeasure

sections.heading: Performance Tips
items: Never create objects inside onDraw() — allocate Paint, Path outside
Use hardware acceleration compatible operations

questions.title: Custom view lifecycle — onLayout vs onMeasure

sections.heading: onMeasure(widthSpec, heightSpec)
items: Called to determine the view's desired size
Receives MeasureSpec constraints from the parent (EXACTLY, AT_MOST, UNSPECIFIED)
Must call setMeasuredDimension(width, height) — failing to do so throws an exception
Called before onLayout

sections.heading: onLayout(changed, left, top, right, bottom)
items: Called to position child views within a ViewGroup
Only relevant for ViewGroup subclasses — not for simple View subclasses
Called after onMeasure — sizes are already known at this point
Uses the measurements from onMeasure to place children using child.layout(l, t, r, b)

sections.heading: In Short
items: onMeasure = how big should the view be?
onLayout = where should children be placed?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Layout inflation is the process of parsing an XML layout file and instantiating the corresponding View objects in memory.

sections.heading: Process
items: LayoutInflater.inflate() is called with a layout resource ID
The XmlPullParser reads the XML file from the compiled resources (already binary)
For each XML tag, LayoutInflater looks up the class name (e.g., TextView, LinearLayout)
It instantiates the class via reflection using the 2-argument constructor (Context, AttributeSet)
Attributes from XML are passed via AttributeSet to the view's constructor
Views are assembled into a hierarchy matching the XML structure

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Custom view tag names must match the fully qualified class name or use the app: prefix
attachToRoot parameter: true attaches immediately; false returns the root view without attaching
Inflation is expensive on the main thread for complex layouts — use async inflation if needed

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: setContentView() inflates the specified layout XML and sets it as the content of the Activity's root view (the DecorView).

sections.heading: What Happens Internally
items: The Activity's Window holds a DecorView (the root of all views)
setContentView() calls the Window's setContentView(), which calls LayoutInflater.inflate()
The inflated hierarchy is attached to a content FrameLayout inside the DecorView
The Activity's layout is now displayed on screen

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Must be called before accessing views (before findViewById or ViewBinding)
Typically called in onCreate() right after super.onCreate()

sections.heading: Why ConstraintLayout
items: Flat view hierarchy — no nested LinearLayouts/RelativeLayouts needed
Better performance: Single-pass measurement in most cases
Highly flexible positioning with constraints between views and parent

sections.heading: Barriers
items: A virtual line that positions itself based on the edge of multiple referenced views
Example: A barrier at the end of two text views — adjusts to the widest one dynamically

sections.heading: Guidelines
items: Fixed virtual lines at a percentage or fixed dp from an edge
Use to align multiple views to a consistent position

sections.heading: Chains
items: A group of views linked by bidirectional constraints
Chain styles: spread (evenly distributed), spread_inside, packed (grouped together)

sections.heading: Groups
items: A set of view IDs that can be shown/hidden together with a single visibility change

questions.title: Remote Views and how to use them

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: RemoteViews is a class that describes a View hierarchy that can be displayed in another process — used for App Widgets and Custom Notifications.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: RemoteViews is serializable and can be passed across process boundaries via Binder
The receiving process (launcher, system UI) inflates and displays the layout
You update views using method calls like setTextViewText(), setImageViewResource() — not direct view references
The system applies these operations in the remote process

sections.heading: Common Uses
items: Home screen App Widgets (AppWidgetProvider)
Custom Notification layouts with RemoteViews for notification content view

sections.heading: Limitations
items: Only a subset of View types are supported (TextView, ImageView, Button, etc.)
Cannot use custom views in RemoteViews

sections.heading: Inner Class (non-static)
items: Holds an implicit reference to the outer class instance
Can access outer class members directly
Risk of memory leak: if the inner class outlives the outer class (e.g., in a background thread), it prevents GC of the outer class
Example: Anonymous Runnable or Listener defined inside an Activity

sections.heading: Static Nested Class
items: No reference to the outer class — standalone
Cannot access outer class non-static members directly
No memory leak risk — does not keep the outer class alive
Example: Static ViewHolder inside RecyclerView.Adapter; static Handler subclasses

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Prefer static nested classes; use WeakReference if you need outer class access

sections.heading: volatile
items: A Java/Kotlin keyword for thread visibility
Guarantees that reads/writes to the variable are always done from/to main memory — not cached in CPU registers
Prevents instruction reordering by the JVM around the variable
Use for flags or references shared between threads (e.g., Singleton double-checked locking)

sections.heading: transient
items: A Java keyword for serialization
Marks a field to be excluded from serialization — not written to the byte stream
Use for fields that should not be persisted: passwords, cached data, UI references

sections.heading: In Short
items: volatile = thread safety (visibility between threads)
transient = serialization exclusion

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ADB (Android Debug Bridge) is a command-line tool for communicating with a real device or emulator for debugging, testing, and management.

sections.heading: Common Commands
items: adb devices – List connected devices
adb install app.apk – Install an app
adb logcat – View device logs
adb shell – Access device shell
adb pull/push – Transfer files

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Works with real devices and emulators
Supports debugging, log viewing, and shell access
Used in CI/CD pipelines for automated testing

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: AAPT (Android Asset Packaging Tool) compiles, packages, and manages app resources during the build process.

sections.heading: Key Responsibilities
items: Compiles XML resources (layouts, strings, drawables) into binary format
Generates the R class (R.java/R.kt) for type-safe resource access in code
Packages resources into the APK or AAB
AAPT2 (current version) splits compile and link steps for incremental builds

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: StrictMode is a developer tool that detects accidental disk/network access on the main thread and potential memory leaks.

sections.heading: Policies
items: ThreadPolicy: Detects disk reads/writes and network calls on the main thread
VmPolicy: Detects memory leaks, unclosed SQLite cursors, and unclosed Closeables

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Use only in debug builds — not in production
Actions on violation: log, toast, dialog, or crash the app

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Lint is a static analysis tool built into Android Studio that scans source code and resources for bugs, performance issues, and best practice violations at compile time.

sections.heading: Key Checks
items: Missing translations in string resources
Incorrect API usage for the target SDK
Performance issues (e.g., unused resources)
Security vulnerabilities
Accessibility issues in layouts

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Runs automatically in Android Studio; can run via ./gradlew lint
Custom lint rules can be written for team-specific checks

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Gradle is the build automation tool used by Android projects. It compiles code, packages resources, manages dependencies, and produces APK/AAB outputs.

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Declarative build scripts in Groovy DSL or Kotlin DSL
Dependency management: downloads and caches libraries from Maven/Google repositories
Build variants: supports debug/release flavors with different configs
Incremental builds: only rebuilds what changed
Plugin ecosystem: Android Gradle Plugin (AGP) integrates Android-specific tasks

sections.heading: Files
items: build.gradle (project-level): Classpath dependencies, Gradle plugin versions for all modules
build.gradle (module-level): Android config (compileSdk, minSdk, targetSdk), dependencies for that module
settings.gradle: Declares module names included in the project
gradle.properties: Project-wide Gradle settings (JVM args, flags, API keys)
local.properties: Machine-local properties (SDK path) — not committed to VCS
gradle-wrapper.properties: Specifies the Gradle version to use for the project

questions.title: How to create a custom task in Gradle?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Custom Gradle tasks let you automate project-specific steps — like copying files, running scripts, or generating code.

sections.heading: Groovy DSL Example
Key Points
items: Tasks can depend on other tasks using dependsOn
Use doFirst{} and doLast{} to add actions
Custom tasks can be typed (Copy, Exec, Zip) or generic

sections.heading: annotationProcessor
items: Java annotation processing — processes annotations at compile time to generate code
Used for Java projects or Java-based annotation processors (e.g., Dagger in Java projects)

sections.heading: kapt (Kotlin Annotation Processing Tool)
items: Kotlin's annotation processing — wraps Java annotation processors for use with Kotlin
Compiles Kotlin to stubs, then runs the Java annotation processor on them — slower
Used for Dagger, Glide, Room in Kotlin projects (older approach)

sections.heading: ksp (Kotlin Symbol Processing)
items: Modern replacement for kapt — processes Kotlin symbols natively without stub generation
Significantly faster than kapt (up to 2x)
Used for Room, Moshi, Dagger-Hilt where KSP support is available
Preferred for new projects

sections.heading: implementation
items: Dependency is available only within the module that declares it
Not exposed to consumers of the module — transitive dependency is hidden
Faster build: dependent modules don't recompile when this dependency changes internally
Use for most dependencies

sections.heading: api
items: Dependency is exposed to consumers of the module — part of the public API
If the dependency changes, all modules depending on this module must also recompile
Use only when the dependency's types appear in the module's public API (e.g., method signatures)

sections.heading: In Short
items: implementation = internal use only (prefer this)
api = leak dependency to consumers (use sparingly)

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Build variants are combinations of Build Types and Product Flavors that let you produce multiple versions of your app from one codebase.

sections.heading: Build Types
items: debug: Debug symbols, logging enabled, debuggable=true
release: Minification, obfuscation (ProGuard/R8), signing config applied

sections.heading: Product Flavors
items: Define different app versions: free vs paid, dev vs staging vs prod
Can have different applicationId, API base URLs, feature flags
Grouped in flavorDimensions for multi-dimensional variants

sections.heading: Result
items: Build Types × Product Flavors = Build Variants
Example: freeDebug, freRelease, paidDebug, paidRelease

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Desugaring is the process by which D8/R8 transforms Java 8+ language features (lambdas, streams, method references) into bytecode compatible with older Android versions.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: D8 replaces javac-generated bytecode for Java 8+ features with equivalent Java 7-compatible code
Core library desugaring extends this to Java 8+ APIs (java.time, java.util.stream) on older API levels
Enabled by adding coreLibraryDesugaringEnabled = true in build.gradle

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Without desugaring, using lambdas or java.time on API <26 would crash at runtime
Desugaring adds a small overhead to build time but enables modern APIs on older devices

sections.heading: Techniques
items: Enable R8/ProGuard minification: removes unused code and resources
Resource shrinking: shrinkResources = true removes unused drawables, layouts, strings
Use Android App Bundle (AAB): Play Store delivers only the code and resources for the device's configuration
Use WebP for images instead of PNG — smaller file size with same quality
Split APKs by ABI: Separate APKs for arm64, x86 instead of one fat APK
Remove unused language resources with resConfigs in build.gradle
Analyze with APK Analyzer in Android Studio to find what's taking space

questions.title: How to speed up Gradle builds?

sections.heading: Techniques
items: Enable Gradle build caching: org.gradle.caching=true in gradle.properties
Enable parallel builds: org.gradle.parallel=true
Increase JVM heap: org.gradle.jvmargs=-Xmx4g
Use configuration on demand: org.gradle.configureondemand=true
Switch from kapt to ksp — significantly faster annotation processing
Use modular architecture — only recompile changed modules
Avoid using api{} — prefer implementation{} to reduce recompilation scope
Keep compileSdk and AGP up to date — newer versions have build performance improvements

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Multiple APKs allow you to publish different APKs targeting different device configurations (ABI, screen density, API level) to reduce APK size per device.

sections.heading: ABI Splits
items: Generate separate APKs for arm64-v8a, armeabi-v7a, x86 — each contains only native .so files for that ABI
Reduces APK size significantly for apps using NDK

sections.heading: Density Splits
items: Separate APKs for hdpi, xhdpi, xxhdpi — each contains only relevant drawable resources

sections.heading: Better Alternative
items: Use Android App Bundle (AAB) — Play Store generates optimized APKs automatically for each device configuration
AAB supersedes manual multi-APK for most cases

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Kotlin DSL is an alternative to Groovy DSL for writing Gradle build scripts using Kotlin syntax.

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Type-safe: IDE provides auto-complete, navigation, and compile-time error checking
Better refactoring support in Android Studio
Consistent language: use Kotlin for both app code and build scripts
build.gradle.kts files replace build.gradle

sections.heading: Disadvantages
items: Slightly slower build script compilation on first run
Migration from Groovy DSL requires syntax changes
Some Groovy-dynamic tricks are not directly available

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Google recommends Kotlin DSL for new projects — the default in new Android Studio projects

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: ProGuard is a tool that shrinks, optimizes, and obfuscates Java/Kotlin bytecode to reduce APK size and protect code from reverse engineering.

sections.heading: What It Does
items: Shrinking: Removes unused classes, methods, and fields
Optimization: Inlines methods, removes dead code
Obfuscation: Renames classes and methods to meaningless short names (a, b, c) — harder to reverse engineer
Pre-verification: Verifies bytecode for the JVM

sections.heading: Key Point
items: ProGuard is replaced by R8 in modern Android builds — R8 does all of the above in a single pass and is faster

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: proguard-rules.pro is a configuration file where you define custom rules that tell ProGuard/R8 what to keep (not shrink or obfuscate) when building a release APK.

sections.heading: Common Rules
items: Keep rules for classes used via reflection (e.g., GSON models, Retrofit interfaces)
Keep rules for third-party libraries that require specific class names
Keep data classes used for JSON deserialization — obfuscation breaks field name matching
Keep names of classes used in AndroidManifest.xml (Activities, Services)

sections.heading: Example
ProGuard
items: Older Java bytecode optimizer/shrinker/obfuscator
Works on .class files after compilation — a separate step
Multi-pass: separate shrink, optimize, obfuscate, pre-verify steps
Slower overall build pipeline

sections.heading: R8
items: Google's replacement for ProGuard in Android builds — enabled by default since AGP 3.4
Works directly on DEX — integrated with D8 compiler in a single pass
Faster builds — combines desugaring, shrinking, and optimization in one step
Compatible with ProGuard rules (reads proguard-rules.pro)
Better dead code elimination and optimization than ProGuard

sections.heading: In Short
items: R8 is the modern default — faster, single-pass, better optimization
ProGuard rules files are still used to configure R8

sections.heading: Minification
items: Removes unused code and resources from the APK — reduces size
Enabled by minifyEnabled = true in build.gradle
R8 removes classes, methods, and fields not reachable from entry points (Activities, etc.)
shrinkResources = true removes unused drawable/layout/string resources

sections.heading: Obfuscation
items: Renames classes, methods, and fields to short meaningless names (a, b, c, aa, etc.)
Makes decompiled code hard to understand — protection against reverse engineering
Mapping file (mapping.txt) generated by R8 lets you de-obfuscate crash stack traces
Upload mapping.txt to Play Console or Crashlytics for readable crash reports

questions.title: Things to take care of while using ProGuard

sections.heading: Common Pitfalls
items: JSON models: Data classes used for GSON/Moshi must be kept — obfuscation breaks field name mapping
Reflection: Classes accessed via reflection must have -keep rules
Third-party libraries: Most popular libs (Retrofit, OkHttp, Glide) include their own consumer ProGuard rules automatically
AndroidManifest entries: Activity, Service, BroadcastReceiver class names must not be obfuscated
Enums: Keep enum classes if accessed by name
Stack traces: Always save the mapping.txt for each release build to decode obfuscated crash reports

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Test the release build before publishing — ProGuard issues only appear in release mode
Use @Keep annotation to protect specific classes/members from obfuscation in Kotlin

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A CI/CD pipeline automates the build, test, and deployment process for Android apps.

sections.heading: Typical Pipeline Steps
items: Trigger: PR or push to main branch triggers the pipeline (GitHub Actions, Bitrise, CircleCI, Jenkins)
Checkout: Clone the repository
Cache dependencies: Cache Gradle files and dependencies to speed up subsequent runs
Build: ./gradlew assembleRelease or bundleRelease
Unit Tests: ./gradlew test
Lint: ./gradlew lint
Instrumented Tests: Run on Firebase Test Lab or emulators
Sign APK/AAB: Apply keystore signing
Deploy: Upload to Play Store internal track via Fastlane or Play Developer API

sections.heading: Common Tools
items: GitHub Actions, Bitrise, CircleCI — CI platforms
Fastlane — automates signing and Play Store deployment

sections.heading: Before Release
items: minifyEnabled = true and shrinkResources = true in release build config
All debug logging removed or guarded by BuildConfig.DEBUG
App is signed with release keystore (never lose the keystore)
versionCode incremented; versionName updated
All API keys are for production (not sandbox/dev keys)
Crashlytics or error tracking configured
Firebase Remote Config set to production values
ProGuard/R8 mapping.txt backed up for crash deobfuscation
GDPR/privacy policy URL accessible
Accessibility review completed — content descriptions, contrast ratios

questions.title: 16 KB page size for Android apps

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Starting with Android 15, some devices use a 16 KB memory page size instead of the traditional 4 KB. Apps using native code (NDK) must be aligned to 16 KB page boundaries to function correctly on these devices.

sections.heading: Impact
