items: Apps with only Java/Kotlin code are not affected
Apps using .so native libraries must be rebuilt and aligned with 16 KB page size
Misaligned .so files will crash on 16 KB page size devices

sections.heading: How to Fix
items: Recompile native code with updated NDK (r28+) and appropriate linker flags
Use the ELF alignment checker to verify .so files
Test on a 16 KB page size emulator or device

questions.title: How to measure method execution time?

sections.heading: Methods
items: System.nanoTime() or SystemClock.elapsedRealtimeNanos(): Most accurate for measuring elapsed time
Kotlin measureTimeMillis{} / measureNanoTime{}: Inline functions that time a block
Android Tracing API: Trace.beginSection("name") / endSection() — visible in Android Studio CPU Profiler and Systrace
Macrobenchmark library: For measuring app startup and UI performance in a realistic environment
Android Studio CPU Profiler: Record traces to see method execution time with flamecharts

questions.title: What is Support Library? Why was it introduced?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The Android Support Library was a set of libraries providing backward-compatible versions of newer Android APIs, allowing modern features on older Android versions.

sections.heading: Why Introduced
items: Android fragmentation: Many devices ran older OS versions
Backward compatibility: RecyclerView, CardView, ViewPager on older APIs
Consistent behavior: Same UI components across Android versions

sections.heading: Current Status
items: Support Library is superseded by AndroidX (Jetpack)
AndroidX uses consistent package naming (androidx.*) and is versioned independently from the OS
All new development should use AndroidX — Support Library is no longer updated

questions.title: Android Push Notification Flow using FCM

sections.heading: Flow
sections.content: 1. App registers with Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM) on first launch. 2. FCM returns a unique device registration token. 3. App sends this token to your backend server. 4. Server stores the token and associates it with the user. 5. When a push is needed, server sends a message to FCM API with the target token. 6. FCM delivers the message to the device. 7. FCM SDK on the device calls FirebaseMessagingService.onMessageReceived(). 8. App displays a notification or processes the data payload.

sections.heading: Token Refresh
items: FCM tokens can change — implement onNewToken() callback in FirebaseMessagingService to update the server when the token changes

questions.title: How does Android Push Notification system work?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android push notifications are delivered via FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) which uses a persistent connection to Google's servers.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: A persistent TCP connection is maintained between the device and Google's FCM servers (even when the app is not running)
This connection is managed by Google Play Services — not the app itself
When FCM receives a message for the device, it pushes it through this connection
The FCM SDK wakes up the app (or its background service) via an Intent
The app processes the message in FirebaseMessagingService.onMessageReceived()

sections.heading: Notification vs Data Messages
items: Notification message: FCM displays it automatically when app is in background
Data message: Always delivered to onMessageReceived() — app handles display

sections.heading: Steps
items: Create an Intent targeting the Activity you want to open
Wrap it in a PendingIntent with FLAG_UPDATE_CURRENT and FLAG_IMMUTABLE
Set the PendingIntent on the NotificationCompat.Builder via .setContentIntent()
Add .setAutoCancel(true) so the notification dismisses on tap
Handle the Intent in the target Activity's onCreate() / onNewIntent() to act on notification data

sections.heading: Back Stack
items: Use TaskStackBuilder to construct an artificial back stack so the user can press back naturally after opening from a notification
Add the parent Activity chain with addParentStack()

questions.title: Push notifications — foreground vs background behavior

sections.heading: App in Foreground
items: FCM delivers the message to FirebaseMessagingService.onMessageReceived() regardless of message type
The system does NOT automatically show a notification — your code must build and display it manually
Data-only messages: always delivered to onMessageReceived()
Notification messages: also go to onMessageReceived() when app is foreground

sections.heading: App in Background / Killed
items: Notification messages: FCM SDK automatically displays the notification in the system tray — no app code runs
Data messages: Delivered to onMessageReceived() when the app is eventually started
When user taps the notification, the launcher Activity is started with the notification payload in the Intent extras

sections.heading: Best Practice
items: Use data-only messages and handle display manually for full control in both states

sections.heading: Doze Mode
items: Activates when device is stationary, screen off, and not charging
Defers background CPU, network, and sync work
Allows maintenance windows periodically where deferred work runs
Apps can request exemption (not recommended for most apps)

sections.heading: App Standby
items: Applies to apps not recently used by the user
Restricts background network and jobs for idle apps
Active usage resets the standby bucket

sections.heading: App Standby Buckets (Android 9+)
items: Active → Working Set → Frequent → Rare — progressively more restricted background work
System uses ML (Adaptive Battery) to classify apps into buckets based on usage patterns

questions.title: How Android implements adaptive battery using ML?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Adaptive Battery uses on-device machine learning (powered by DeepMind) to predict which apps the user is unlikely to use in the next few hours and restricts their background activity.

sections.heading: How It Works
items: The system learns the user's app usage patterns over time
Apps are classified into standby buckets: Active, Working Set, Frequent, Rare, Restricted
Apps in lower buckets (Rare, Restricted) have their background jobs, alarms, and network access increasingly limited
The ML model runs entirely on-device — no data sent to Google

sections.heading: Impact on Developers
items: Background jobs may be deferred or rate-limited for less-used apps
Use high-priority FCM for time-sensitive notifications — they bypass Doze
Use WorkManager with Constraints — it works within the system's scheduling policies

questions.title: How to reduce battery usage in an Android app?

sections.heading: Key Strategies
items: Minimize wake locks: Only hold WakeLocks for as long as needed; release immediately after use
Batch network requests: Make multiple calls in one network session instead of many small ones
Use WorkManager with constraints: Run work only when charging or on Wi-Fi
Avoid polling: Use push notifications (FCM) or server-sent events instead of regular polling
Use JobScheduler/WorkManager for deferred work — system batches jobs from multiple apps for efficiency
Reduce GPS usage: Use fused location provider; request low-power modes when precision isn't critical
Avoid running services indefinitely: Use Foreground Service only when truly needed

questions.title: Support different screen sizes and densities

sections.heading: Screen Sizes
items: Use ConstraintLayout for flexible, responsive layouts
Provide alternative layouts in res/layout-sw600dp/ for tablets
Use WindowSizeClass (Jetpack) to adapt layouts to compact/medium/expanded windows

sections.heading: Densities
items: Use dp (density-independent pixels) for dimensions — never px
Provide drawables in res/drawable-mdpi/, -hdpi, -xhdpi, -xxhdpi, -xxxhdpi
Or use vector drawables (SVG) which scale perfectly to any density

sections.heading: Other
items: Use sp for text sizes — respects user font size preferences
Test on multiple screen sizes using the Layout Inspector and emulators
Use adaptive icons for launcher icons

sections.heading: Normal
items: Low-risk permissions granted automatically at install
No user prompt required
Example: INTERNET, ACCESS_NETWORK_STATE, VIBRATE

sections.heading: Dangerous
items: Access to sensitive user data — requires explicit user approval at runtime
Must call requestPermissions() and handle the result
Example: READ_CONTACTS, ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION, CAMERA, READ_CALL_LOG

sections.heading: Signature
items: Granted only to apps signed with the same certificate as the app that declared the permission
Used for IPC between apps from the same developer
Not user-facing

sections.heading: SignatureOrSystem
items: Granted to apps signed with the same certificate OR apps installed in the system image
Restricted to system-level use

questions.title: What is the NDK and why is it useful?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: The NDK (Native Development Kit) allows you to implement parts of your Android app in C or C++.

sections.heading: Why It Is Useful
items: Performance: C/C++ can be faster for CPU-intensive tasks (signal processing, physics, compression)
Reuse existing libraries: Port existing C/C++ codebases (game engines, codec libraries) to Android
Low-level hardware access: Direct memory management, SIMD instructions
Cross-platform: Share C/C++ code between Android and iOS

sections.heading: Key Points
items: NDK code runs in a separate .so native library loaded by the JVM via System.loadLibrary()
JNI (Java Native Interface) bridges Java/Kotlin and native code
Use NDK only when truly needed — most apps don't need it

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: RenderScript was a framework for running computationally intensive tasks in parallel on the CPU or GPU, primarily for image processing and data-parallel computations.

sections.heading: Key Points
items: Provided a C99-based scripting language for parallel computation
Used for blurring images, applying filters, matrix operations
Automatically utilized multiple CPU cores and the GPU
Deprecated in Android 12 — removed in subsequent releases

sections.heading: Replacement
items: Use Vulkan for GPU compute workloads
Use RenderEffect API (Android 12+) for image effects
Use GPGPU via OpenCL or NNAPI for ML inference

questions.title: How the OS handles process flow — when is app killed?

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Android manages app processes using a priority hierarchy. When memory is low, the system kills processes starting from the lowest priority.

sections.heading: Process Priority (High to Low)
items: Foreground: App is active and visible — never killed unless critical
Visible: Partially visible (e.g., behind a dialog)
Service: Background service running — killed under memory pressure
Background: App is invisible and stopped — can be killed anytime
Empty: No active components — first to be killed to reclaim memory

sections.heading: When App Is Killed
items: System never kills the foreground process unless absolutely necessary
Android may also kill processes to enforce battery optimization (Doze, App Standby)
When the user removes the app from Recents, onDestroy() is called if possible, then the process is killed

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Espresso is Google's official UI testing framework for Android. It enables writing concise, reliable instrumented tests that interact with UI components on a device or emulator.

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Automatically synchronizes with the UI thread — no Thread.sleep() needed
Fluent API: onView(matcher).perform(action).check(assertion)
Built-in matchers (withId, withText) and actions (click, typeText, scrollTo)
Integrates with Android Test Runner for CI

sections.heading: Example
items: onView(withId(R.id.button)).perform(click())
onView(withId(R.id.textView)).check(matches(withText("Hello")))

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Robolectric is a testing framework that lets you run Android tests on the JVM without a device or emulator by providing shadow implementations of Android classes.

sections.heading: Advantages
items: Fast: Tests run on JVM — no emulator boot time
Can test Android-dependent code (Context, Activity, etc.) in unit tests
Integrated with JUnit 4/5

sections.heading: Disadvantages
items: Shadow implementations may not perfectly match real Android behavior — tests may pass but fail on a real device
Not suitable for testing real GPU rendering or complex hardware interactions
Can be slow to configure for complex tests
Incomplete support for newer Android APIs — shadows may lag behind OS releases

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: UI Automator is a testing framework for writing instrumented tests that interact with the system UI and other apps — not just your own app.

sections.heading: Key Features
items: Can test interactions that cross app boundaries (e.g., share sheet, notifications, Settings app)
Works with any app — uses accessibility services to find and interact with UI elements
Complements Espresso: Espresso for within-app testing, UI Automator for cross-app or system UI

sections.heading: Example Use Cases
items: Test notification behavior after your app sends one
Test file picker or permission dialogs
Integration tests involving system settings

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: A unit test verifies the behavior of a single, isolated unit of code (a function, class, or module) without external dependencies.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Fast: Runs on the JVM — no device or emulator needed
Isolated: Dependencies are replaced with mocks or fakes
Deterministic: Same input always produces same output
Scope: Tests one function or class in isolation

sections.heading: Tools in Android
items: JUnit 4/5 — test framework
Mockito / MockK — mocking frameworks
Turbine — testing Kotlin Flow
Truth / AssertJ — readable assertions

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: An instrumented test runs on a real Android device or emulator and has access to the full Android framework, including UI, Context, and system services.

sections.heading: Key Characteristics
items: Slower than unit tests — requires device/emulator boot
Can test UI interactions, database operations, and system services
Runs against the actual Android runtime

sections.heading: Tools
items: Espresso: UI interaction testing
AndroidJUnit4: Test runner for instrumented tests
Room in-memory database for testing Room DAOs
Hilt testing support for dependency injection in tests

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Mockito is a Java/Kotlin mocking framework used to create mock objects in unit tests — simulating dependencies without requiring real implementations.

sections.heading: Why It Is Used
items: Isolate the unit under test: Replace real dependencies (Repository, API, DB) with controlled mocks
Verify behavior: Assert that certain methods were called with specific arguments
Stub responses: Define what the mock should return when a method is called
Avoid side effects: No network calls or DB writes in unit tests

sections.heading: MockK (Kotlin)
items: MockK is the Kotlin-native alternative to Mockito — better coroutine support, cleaner DSL
Preferred for Kotlin projects

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Code coverage measures what percentage of your source code is executed during testing.

sections.heading: Types of Coverage
items: Line coverage: Percentage of code lines executed by tests
Branch coverage: Percentage of code branches (if/else paths) executed
Method coverage: Percentage of methods called

sections.heading: Key Points
items: High coverage doesn't guarantee correct tests — tests must also assert correctly
Target ~80% coverage for business logic; 100% is impractical
Use JaCoCo in Gradle to generate coverage reports
Coverage report: ./gradlew testDebugUnitTestCoverage

sections.heading: Stub
items: A minimal implementation that returns predefined responses to method calls
Does not verify that methods were called — only provides canned answers
Used when you just need the dependency to return something so the test can proceed
Example: Stub repository always returns an empty list

sections.heading: Mock
items: A test double that records how it was called and verifies interactions
Used to assert that specific methods were called with specific arguments
Created with Mockito.mock() or mockk{}
Combines stubbing (what to return) with verification (was it called?)

sections.heading: In Short
items: Stub = fake data provider (state-based testing)
Mock = interaction verifier (behavior-based testing)

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: TDD (Test-Driven Development) is a practice where tests are written before the implementation: Red (failing test) → Green (make it pass) → Refactor.

sections.heading: Challenges in Android
items: Android dependency: Activities, Context, Views are hard to unit test — requires Robolectric or instrumented tests
Slow feedback loop: Instrumented tests on device/emulator are slow — breaks TDD rhythm
Legacy code: Existing tightly coupled code is hard to retrofit with tests
Learning curve: Teams unfamiliar with mocking and testing patterns
UI testing fragility: Espresso tests can be flaky depending on timing and device state

sections.heading: How to Overcome
items: Use MVVM/Clean Architecture — keeps business logic in pure Kotlin classes, easy to unit test
Use MockK/Mockito to mock Android dependencies
Use Hilt testing support for DI in tests

questions.title: How can two distinct Android apps interact?

sections.heading: Methods
items: Intents: Start an Activity or Service in another app using explicit or implicit Intents
Content Providers: Share structured data between apps via URI-based access with permissions
Broadcast Receivers: Send broadcasts that other apps can listen to
AIDL (Android Interface Definition Language): Define a cross-process interface for direct method calls via Binder
Messenger: Simplified IPC via Handler-based message passing over Binder
Shared Files: Both apps read/write to the same file via FileProvider with proper permissions

sections.heading: Options
items: Coroutines + Dispatchers.IO: Best for lightweight async work tied to a lifecycle (ViewModel, Activity)
WorkManager: Persistent, guaranteed background work — survives app death and device reboots
Foreground Service: Long-running user-visible work (music, GPS, file upload)
JobScheduler: System-scheduled work with battery-efficient constraints (superseded by WorkManager)
AlarmManager: Exact-time alarms (use sparingly; use WorkManager for deferrable work)
Service (plain): Background work without UI — runs on main thread by default; needs manual thread management

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: IPC in Android is primarily handled by the Binder mechanism — a kernel-level driver enabling fast, lightweight message passing between processes.

sections.heading: IPC Mechanisms
items: Binder: Android's primary IPC — used internally by Services, ContentProviders, AIDL
AIDL: Define an interface for cross-process method calls — Binder handles marshalling/unmarshalling
Messenger: Simplified Binder-based IPC using Message objects and Handlers
ContentProvider: Share data between processes via query/insert/update/delete URI contracts
Broadcast Intents: One-to-many messaging across processes

sections.heading: Key Point
items: All Android IPC ultimately goes through Binder — even high-level APIs like startActivity() use Binder to communicate with ActivityManagerService

sections.heading: Activity to Activity
items: Pass data via Intent extras when starting the Activity
Use startActivityForResult() / ActivityResultLauncher for returning results

sections.heading: Activity to Fragment
items: Use Fragment.newInstance() with a Bundle for initial data
Share a ViewModel scoped to the Activity — both Activity and Fragment observe it
Call fragment.arguments for one-time setup data

sections.heading: Fragment to Fragment
items: Shared ViewModel (scoped to Activity or NavGraph): Both Fragments observe the same ViewModel — preferred approach
Fragment Result API: setFragmentResult() / setFragmentResultListener() — for one-time results
Avoid direct Fragment-to-Fragment references — tight coupling, memory leak risk

sections.heading: Deep Link
items: A URL that navigates the user to a specific screen inside the app
If the app is not installed, the link opens the Play Store or a fallback web URL
Implemented via intent-filter in AndroidManifest.xml with action VIEW and a scheme/host pattern
Example: myapp://product/123 or https://myapp.com/product/123 (App Link)

sections.heading: Dynamic Link (Firebase)
items: A smart URL that works whether the app is installed or not
If installed: Opens the app to the correct screen
If not installed: Sends to Play Store; after install and first open, navigates to the intended screen (deferred deep link)
Platform-independent: Same link works on Android, iOS, and web

sections.heading: How Deeplinking Works
items: Declare an intent-filter in AndroidManifest.xml with the scheme and host
The OS matches the URL to the app and starts the Activity with the URL in the Intent
In onCreate()/onNewIntent(), parse the Intent data (Uri) to extract path parameters
Navigate to the correct screen using the extracted parameters

sections.heading: Jetpack Navigation Deep Links
items: Define deep links in the navigation graph XML — NavController handles routing automatically
Supports both explicit deeplinking (PendingIntent) and implicit (intent-filter)

sections.heading: Handling Edge Cases
items: App already running: Override onNewIntent() to handle deeplinks when app is in background
Authenticated routes: Check if user is logged in before navigating to protected screens
Fallback: If deeplink path doesn't match, navigate to home screen

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Parcelable is Android's high-performance serialization mechanism for passing objects between components (Activities, Fragments, Services) via Bundles and Intents.

sections.heading: How It Works Internally
items: A Parcel is a container for a flattened (marshalled) message — writes to a shared memory block
writeToParcel() serializes the object's fields into the Parcel in order
CREATOR.createFromParcel() reads fields in the same order to reconstruct the object
No reflection — reads/writes are direct field-by-field operations — very fast
Data is passed via Binder (kernel memory copy) for IPC, or via the Bundle in the same process

sections.heading: @Parcelize
items: Kotlin annotation that auto-generates writeToParcel() and CREATOR at compile time — no boilerplate

questions.title: Serializable vs Parcelable — disadvantages of Serializable

sections.heading: Serializable (Java)
items: Interface with no methods — Java handles serialization automatically via reflection
Simple to implement: just add implements Serializable
Uses reflection: slow, creates many temporary objects → GC pressure
Not Android-optimized — designed for Java object persistence to disk/network

sections.heading: Parcelable (Android)
items: Android-specific, optimized for IPC and in-process data passing
Manual serialization: developer writes writeToParcel() and createFromParcel() — no reflection
Significantly faster than Serializable — fewer allocations
Preferred for passing data between Android components

sections.heading: In Short
items: Serializable = simple but slow (reflection)
Parcelable = fast but more code (use @Parcelize to eliminate boilerplate)

questions.title: What happens if Serializable class has a non-serializable member?

sections.heading: Answer
sections.content: If a Serializable class contains a field of a non-Serializable type, serialization will throw a NotSerializableException at runtime when the object is serialized.

sections.heading: Solutions
items: Mark the field as transient: Excludes it from serialization — field will be null after deserialization
Make the referenced class implement Serializable
Use a custom readObject()/writeObject() to handle the field manually

sections.heading: Key Point
items: Static fields are never serialized — they belong to the class, not the instance
transient fields are skipped during serialization and restored to their default value on deserialization

sections.heading: Contract
sections.content: In Java/Kotlin, equals() and hashCode() must be implemented consistently: if two objects are equal (equals() returns true), they must have the same hashCode().

sections.heading: Why It Matters
items: HashMap, HashSet use hashCode() to find the bucket, then equals() to find the exact object
Breaking the contract causes objects to be lost in hash-based collections
If you override equals() you MUST also override hashCode()

sections.heading: Kotlin data class
items: Kotlin data class auto-generates equals() and hashCode() based on all constructor properties — use data class whenever possible

sections.heading: Manual Implementation
items: hashCode(): Use Objects.hash(field1, field2) or combine fields with prime multiplication
equals(): Check reference equality first → null check → type check → field-by-field comparison

questions.title: Android Bundle and Dynamic Feature usage

sections.heading: Android App Bundle (AAB)
items: Publishing format (not installable directly) that lets Google Play generate optimized APKs per device configuration
Play delivers only the native libraries, resources, and language packs needed for that device
Reduces download and install size significantly
Required for new apps on Play Store (since August 2021)

sections.heading: Dynamic Feature Modules
items: Optional parts of your app that are downloaded on demand — not bundled in the base install
User downloads the feature only when they need it (e.g., a scanner, an AR feature)
Implemented using the Play Feature Delivery library
Reduces initial install size; features load seamlessly after download

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Instant Apps allow users to run a lightweight version of your Android app without installing it — accessed via a URL link.

sections.heading: How to Achieve It
items: Modularize your app into a base module and feature modules
Mark feature modules as instant-enabled in the manifest
Keep the instant module small (under 4MB base, 15MB feature modules)
Define URL deep links for each instant-enabled activity
Test with the Instant Apps testing tool in Android Studio

sections.heading: Use Cases
items: Letting users try a feature before installing
Onboarding flows via a shared link
E-commerce checkout without full app install

questions.title: Retain data on orientation change — lifecycle during rotation

sections.heading: Lifecycle on Rotation
sections.content: On rotation: onPause → onStop → onDestroy → onCreate → onStart → onResume (new instance created).

sections.heading: How to Retain Data
items: ViewModel: Survives rotation — best for UI-related data (list items, user input state)
onSaveInstanceState(Bundle): Save small UI state (scroll position, selected tab) — restored in onCreate() or onRestoreInstanceState()
DataStore/SharedPreferences/Database: Persisted to disk — survives everything including process death

sections.heading: Anti-patterns
items: android:configChanges: Handles rotation manually — discourages proper lifecycle handling
Keeping large data in Bundle: Bundle has size limits; large objects may cause TransactionTooLargeException

questions.title: Handle different screen sizes, density, languages, and orientation

sections.heading: Screen Sizes
items: Use ConstraintLayout for adaptive layouts
Alternative layouts in res/layout-sw600dp/ (tablets), layout-w720dp/ (landscape)

sections.heading: Densities
items: Use dp for all dimensions; sp for text
Vector drawables scale perfectly to any density
Provide bitmap drawables in mdpi/hdpi/xhdpi/xxhdpi/xxxhdpi if needed

sections.heading: Languages
items: All strings in res/values/strings.xml
Translations in res/values-fr/, values-ar/, etc.
Use getString() for all user-visible text — never hardcode

sections.heading: Orientation
items: Use ViewModel to survive rotation
Use android:screenOrientation only if strictly needed — lock orientation prevents flexible use

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Process death occurs when the Android OS kills the app's process to reclaim memory — even when the app is in the background with activities in the back stack.

sections.heading: What Happens
items: The app process is killed — all in-memory state is lost
ViewModel data is lost (it is tied to the process)
Next time the user returns to the app, it is re-launched from scratch
The OS preserves the back stack entry but creates a new Activity instance
The Activity receives a restored Bundle via savedInstanceState in onCreate()

sections.heading: How to Handle
items: Use onSaveInstanceState() to save critical UI state
Use SavedStateHandle in ViewModel — persists across process death
Use Room/DataStore for data that must survive — disk-backed

sections.heading: Testing
items: Use 'Don't keep activities' in Developer Options to simulate process death

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Firebase is Google's mobile and web application development platform offering a suite of backend and analytics services.

sections.heading: Key Services
items: Firebase Cloud Messaging (FCM): Push notifications to Android/iOS
Firestore / Realtime Database: Cloud-hosted NoSQL databases with real-time sync
Firebase Auth: Authentication (email, Google, Facebook, phone)
Firebase Crashlytics: Real-time crash reporting with stack traces
Firebase Analytics: User behavior and event tracking
Firebase Remote Config: Update app behavior without app update
Firebase Performance Monitoring: Track startup time, HTTP requests, screen rendering
Firebase A/B Testing: Run experiments on user groups

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.content: Git is a distributed version control system for tracking code changes, collaborating with teams, and managing release history.

sections.heading: Key Commands
items: git clone / init: Start a repository
git add / commit: Stage and save changes
git push / pull: Sync with remote
git branch / checkout / merge: Branching and merging
git rebase: Rewrite commit history for a clean linear log
git stash: Temporarily shelf uncommitted changes
git log / diff / blame: Inspect history and changes

sections.heading: Android Team Workflow
items: Feature branches from main/develop
PRs with code review before merging
Tags for release versions
.gitignore: Exclude build/, .idea/, local.properties, keystore files
